units.title: Unit 1 — Application of Media and Information Communications Technology

topics.title: Hardware
topics.description: Computer systems, components, operating systems, and choosing the right hardware

blocks.text: Every digital device is built on hardware — the physical components that process, store, and move data. Before we can understand how a computer works, we need to understand the difference between hardware and software.

items.label: 🔧 Hardware
points: The physical components of a computer
Anything you can physically touch
Examples: CPU, RAM, keyboard, monitor
Cannot be changed without replacing parts
Without hardware, nothing runs

items.label: 💾 Software
points: Programs and code that run on hardware
Cannot be physically touched
Examples: Windows, Chrome, Photoshop
Can be updated, installed, or deleted
Software tells hardware what to do

blocks.heading: How a computer processes information
blocks.text: Every computer follows the same four-step cycle: Input → Process → Store → Output. Input devices (keyboard, mouse) send data in. The CPU processes it. Storage holds results. Output devices (monitor, speakers) present them. Every task — from loading a webpage to editing a photo — follows this pattern.

blocks.title: How does Computer Hardware Work?
blocks.caption: 3D animated teardown — see exactly what's inside a laptop and what each part does

items.label: Desktop
items.description: High performance, stays in one location, easily upgraded. Two types: Tower (separate monitor and case) and All-in-One (AIO, monitor and components in one unit).

items.label: Mobile Devices
items.description: Laptops, tablets, smartphones, and wearables (smartwatches). Battery-powered and portable, but harder to upgrade.

items.label: Server
items.description: Powerful computers that store and share files, run applications, and host websites for other devices on a network. Designed for reliability, not user interaction.

blocks.heading: The Central Processing Unit (CPU)
blocks.html:
The CPU is the brain of the computer — every instruction, calculation, and decision runs through it.

The CPU has two main internal components: the ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) performs all mathematical and logical operations (add, subtract, compare). The Control Unit directs how data and instructions flow through the CPU — it fetches, decodes, and executes every instruction.

The CPU works by repeating the Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle: it fetches an instruction from memory, decodes what it means, then executes it. This happens billions of times per second.

blocks.title: How a CPU Works
blocks.caption: A clear walkthrough of exactly what happens inside a processor

items.label: Clock Speed (GHz)
items.description: How many instruction cycles the CPU completes per second. A 4 GHz CPU completes 4 billion cycles per second. Higher = faster.

items.label: Core Count
items.description: Each core can handle tasks independently. A quad-core CPU runs 4 tasks simultaneously. More cores = better multitasking.

items.label: Cache Memory
items.description: A tiny, ultra-fast memory built into the CPU itself. Stores frequently used data so the CPU doesn't have to wait for slower RAM. Bigger cache = faster performance.

blocks.heading: CPU analogy
blocks.text: Think of the CPU as a school. The Control Unit is the principal — directing what happens and when. The ALU is the maths teacher — doing all the calculations. Cache is the teacher's desk with today's materials ready to grab. RAM is the classroom — where work happens. Storage is the filing cabinet — everything saved for later.

Computers use a hierarchy of memory types — from ultra-fast cache inside the CPU down to permanent storage. The faster the memory, the smaller and more expensive it is. Understanding each type is essential.

items.label: Cache Memory
items.description: Built into the CPU itself. Extremely fast, tiny capacity (MB). Stores data the CPU is using right now. The CPU checks cache first before going to RAM.

items.label: Primary Memory (RAM)
items.description: Fast but volatile — lost when power off. Holds the OS, open apps, and active data. Directly accessed by the CPU. Measured in GB (e.g. 16 GB).

items.label: Secondary Storage
items.description: Permanent — survives power off. Much larger capacity (TB). HDD, SSD, USB drives. Slower to access. Stores the OS, apps, and all your files.

items.label: ⚡ Primary Memory
points: Directly accessed by the CPU
RAM — fast, volatile (lost when powered off)
ROM — stores firmware (BIOS), non-volatile
Limited capacity (e.g. 16–64 GB RAM)
Much faster than secondary storage

items.label: 💾 Secondary Storage
points: Permanent — data survives power off
HDD, SSD, USB drives, SD cards
Much larger capacity (e.g. 1–4 TB)
Slower to access than RAM
Stores the OS, apps, and your files

blocks.title: SSD vs HDD — What's the Difference?
blocks.caption: Understand the real trade-offs between speed, cost, and durability

items.label: 🌀 HDD (Hard Disk Drive)
points: Spinning magnetic platters
Slower read/write speeds
Cheaper per gigabyte
Higher capacities available
Vulnerable to physical damage

items.label: ⚡ SSD (Solid State Drive)
points: Flash memory — no moving parts
Much faster — boots in seconds
More expensive per gigabyte
Durable and energy efficient
Standard in all modern devices

blocks.heading: RAM vs Storage — a common confusion
blocks.text: RAM is not the same as storage. RAM holds what the computer is currently doing — close an app and that data is gone. Storage (HDD/SSD) holds everything permanently. More RAM means smoother multitasking. More storage means more files.

blocks.heading: How the CPU Interacts with Memory
blocks.html:
When you start a program, the CPU loads its instructions from storage (HDD/SSD) into RAM. The CPU then executes the program from RAM, which is much faster than reading from storage.

The CPU checks cache memory first — if the data it needs is already there, it can act immediately without waiting for slower RAM. This is why cache size matters for performance.

When you save a file, the CPU writes data from RAM back to storage, where it persists even after the computer is turned off.

blocks.text: Data enters a computer through input devices and leaves through output devices. Any external device connected to a computer is called a peripheral.

items.label: ⌨️ Input Devices
points: Keyboard — text and commands
Mouse / trackpad — cursor control
Microphone — audio input
Webcam — live video capture
Scanner — digitise physical documents
Digital/video camera — image capture
Graphics tablet — precision drawing

items.label: 🖥️ Output Devices
points: Monitor — visual display
Speakers — audio output
Headphones — personal audio
Printer — physical document output

blocks.heading: Peripheral Devices
blocks.html: A peripheral is any external device connected to a computer that provides input, output, or extra functionality. They connect via USB, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, or HDMI.

items.label: Multi-function Device
items.description: Combines printer, scanner, and photocopier in one unit. Reduces device clutter in offices.

items.label: Microphone
items.description: Captures audio for recording, video calls, voice recognition, and streaming.

items.label: Speakers
items.description: Output audio from the system — music, video, alerts, and calls.

items.label: Webcam
items.description: Captures live video for conferencing, streaming, and online teaching.

items.label: Digital/Video Camera
items.description: High-quality still and video capture for media production projects.

items.label: Graphics Tablet
items.description: Pen-based input for precision digital drawing and design work.

blocks.heading: Match peripherals to your task
blocks.text: A graphic designer needs a colour-accurate monitor and a graphics tablet. A podcaster needs a quality condenser microphone. A video editor needs a large, fast external drive. Always choose peripherals based on what you're creating.
An operating system (OS) is the fundamental software that manages all hardware and software on a computer. Without an OS, no application can run — it's the layer between you and the machine.

blocks.title: How Do Operating Systems Work?
blocks.caption: An animated explanation of what an OS actually does behind the scenes

blocks.title: Types of Operating System by Device

items.label: 🖥️ Desktop OS
points: Designed for desktop and laptop computers
Full GUI with windows, icons, menus
Examples: Windows 11, macOS, Linux Ubuntu
Optimised for keyboard and mouse input
Runs complex software and multitasks heavily

items.label: 📱 Mobile OS
points: Designed for smartphones and tablets
Touch-based interface
Examples: Android (Google), iOS (Apple)
Optimised for battery life and portability
Apps distributed via app stores

items.label: Windows
items.description: Most widely used desktop OS. Huge software compatibility. Vulnerable to malware without good security practices.

items.label: macOS
items.description: Apple's desktop OS. Seamless with Apple devices. High security. Limited hardware choice and more expensive.

items.label: iOS
items.description: Apple's mobile OS. Strong security, smooth interface. Restricted to Apple devices — no customisation outside App Store.

items.label: Android
items.description: Google's mobile OS. Highly customisable, runs on many brands. Can have security vulnerabilities on older, unpatched devices.

items.label: Linux
items.description: Free, open-source OS. Dominates servers. Excellent security and stability. Steeper learning curve for beginners.

items.label: User Interface
items.description: Provides a GUI (icons, windows, menus) or CLI (text commands) for interacting with the computer.

items.label: Managing Resources
items.description: Allocates CPU time, RAM, and storage between running applications so they don't conflict.

items.label: Security & Access
items.description: Controls user accounts, passwords, and file permissions to protect data from unauthorised access.

items.label: Running Applications
items.description: Loads programs into memory and manages communication between software and hardware.

blocks.text: Knowing how to diagnose hardware problems and select the right hardware for a task are practical skills assessed in AIT. This lesson covers software compatibility, common faults, and how to choose hardware for a purpose.

blocks.heading: Software Compatibility
blocks.html:
Software compatibility means whether a program can run correctly on your specific hardware and OS. Two common problems arise:

Older software on new hardware: Legacy 32-bit apps may not work on modern 64-bit systems. Compatibility modes or virtualisation can sometimes help.

Newer software on old hardware: Modern software lists minimum hardware requirements. If your machine doesn't meet them, the software won't install or run. Example: Windows 11 requires a TPM 2.0 chip that many older computers don't have.

blocks.heading: Always check minimum requirements
blocks.text: Before buying or installing software, check the developer's minimum hardware requirements — CPU, RAM, storage, and OS version. Meeting only the minimum usually means sluggish performance. Aim for the recommended specs.

blocks.heading: Troubleshooting Common Faults
blocks.html: Troubleshooting is systematic — always start simple (restart, check cables) before trying complex solutions.

items.label: Won't Start
items.description: Check power cable connections, try another outlet, check the power supply unit.

items.label: Running Slowly
items.description: Check RAM usage, close background apps, check available storage, scan for malware.

items.label: Device Not Recognised
items.description: Check the connection type, update or reinstall drivers, try a different USB port.

items.label: No Internet
items.description: Check cable or Wi-Fi signal, restart the router, check IP configuration.

blocks.heading: Selecting hardware for a purpose
blocks.text: Match hardware to the task. Video editing needs a fast multi-core CPU, 32 GB+ RAM, and an SSD. A basic document workstation needs far less. Always verify your chosen hardware meets the minimum requirements of all software you plan to run.

items.definition: Central Processing Unit — executes all program instructions; measured in GHz and number of cores.
Random Access Memory — fast, volatile primary memory for active data and programs.
Memory directly accessed by the CPU: RAM (volatile) and ROM (non-volatile).
Permanent storage not directly accessed by the CPU — HDD, SSD, USB drives.
Software that manages all hardware and software, providing a user interface and running applications.
An external device providing input, output, or extra functionality (e.g. printer, webcam, microphone).
Graphical User Interface — icons, windows, and menus, as opposed to a text-based CLI.
Whether software can run correctly on a given hardware and OS configuration.

question.text: Which type of memory is volatile and loses its contents when the computer is powered off?
A school cannot install Windows 11 on older computers because they lack a TPM 2.0 chip. This is an example of:
options: Newer software not meeting the minimum hardware requirements of older hardware

question.text: Which function of an operating system controls user accounts, passwords, and file permissions?
What is the purpose of CPU cache memory?
options: To permanently store the operating system
To hold the user's saved files between sessions
To provide ultra-fast temporary storage for frequently accessed CPU instructions and data
To connect the CPU to wireless networks

question.text: What is the role of the BIOS/UEFI in a computer system?
options: It manages user accounts and file permissions
It initialises hardware at startup and loads the operating system
It processes all graphical output to the display
It permanently stores the user's documents and files

question.text: Which advantage does a Solid State Drive (SSD) have over a Hard Disk Drive (HDD)?
options: SSDs have a higher storage capacity for the same price
SSDs produce louder operating sounds, helping identify activity
SSDs have significantly faster read/write speeds and no moving parts
SSDs are always more reliable over a 10-year period

question.text: What happens to data stored in RAM when the computer is turned off?
options: It is automatically saved to the hard drive
It is backed up to cloud storage
It is permanently lost because RAM is volatile memory
It remains in memory until the next startup

question.text: Which of the following is BOTH an input and an output device?
A video editor needs to run multiple demanding programs at once without slowdown. Which CPU feature is most important for this task?
options: A very high clock speed above 5 GHz
A large amount of cache memory

question.text: A gaming computer has an extremely powerful GPU but very slow RAM. What performance problem does this create?
Explain the difference between primary memory and secondary storage. Give one example of each. (4 marks)
question.explanation:
Primary memory is directly accessible by the CPU and stores data and instructions currently in use. It is fast but volatile — contents are lost when power is removed. Example: RAM. (2 marks)

Secondary storage holds data permanently and retains it without power. It has much greater capacity than primary memory but is slower to access. Example: SSD or HDD. (2 marks)

question.text: Describe two functions of an operating system and explain why each is important. (4 marks)
question.explanation:
Any two of:

Managing system resources: The OS allocates CPU time, RAM, and storage between running applications so they can operate efficiently without conflicting. Without this, multiple programs could attempt to use the same resources simultaneously, causing crashes. (2 marks)

Managing security and access rights: The OS controls system access through user accounts, passwords, and file permissions, preventing unauthorised users from accessing or modifying data. This is essential in shared or networked environments. (2 marks)

User interface: The OS provides a GUI or CLI so users can interact with the computer and its software. Without this, users would have no way to issue commands or use applications. (2 marks)

Running applications: The OS loads programs into memory and allocates the resources they need. Without OS management, software could not communicate with hardware or run reliably. (2 marks)

question.text: A graphic design studio is purchasing new computers for its designers. Describe the criteria they should consider when selecting hardware and explain how software compatibility should influence their decision. (8 marks)
question.explanation:
Hardware selection criteria (5 marks):

CPU: Design software such as Adobe Photoshop and Illustrator is CPU-intensive. The studio should select computers with a modern multi-core processor (e.g. 8+ cores at high GHz) to handle rendering and processing large files without lag.

RAM: Working with high-resolution images and multiple open files demands significant RAM. A minimum of 16 GB is recommended, with 32 GB preferred for complex projects involving video or 3D elements.

Storage: An SSD is essential for fast file loading and saving. Given the large file sizes common in design work, high-capacity storage (1 TB+) or supplementary external storage should also be considered.

Display: A high-resolution monitor with accurate colour reproduction (wide colour gamut, factory colour-calibrated) is critical for design work where colour accuracy is essential for both screen and print outputs.

Budget: All choices must be balanced against the studio's available budget.

Software compatibility (3 marks):

The studio must check the minimum and recommended hardware requirements for their software (e.g. Adobe Creative Cloud specifies minimum RAM, OS version, and GPU). Purchasing hardware that only meets minimum requirements may result in poor performance under real workloads. The chosen operating system (macOS or Windows) must be supported by the software versions in use. If the studio uses any specialist or legacy software, they must verify it is compatible with the new OS, as older applications may not function correctly on current operating systems without compatibility tools or virtualisation.

topics.title: Project Management
topics.description: Planning, managing, and evaluating digital media projects

blocks.text: Every professional digital project starts with a design brief. Without one, you're guessing at what the client wants — and that's how projects fail, miss deadlines, and go over budget.

items.label: Purpose
items.description: What must the product do? What problem does it solve or message does it communicate?

items.label: Target Audience
items.description: Who is the product for? Demographics (age, gender, income) and psychographics (values, interests, lifestyle) — these shape every design decision.

items.label: Constraints
items.description: Time, budget, technical limits. What are the boundaries the solution must stay within?

items.label: Deliverables
items.description: What exactly must be produced? Formats, file types, quantities, and dimensions.

blocks.heading: Understanding Your Target Audience
blocks.html:
Effective design requires deep understanding of who will use or view the product. Audience analysis uses two lenses:

Demographics: measurable characteristics — age, gender, income, education level, location, occupation. Demographics tell you WHO your audience is.

Psychographics: values, attitudes, interests, lifestyle, and personality (sometimes called VALS — Values, Attitudes, and Lifestyle). Psychographics tell you WHY your audience makes decisions and what will resonate with them.

Example: A campaign for a community sports club might target demographics of "adults aged 25–45 in the local suburb" but psychographics of "active, family-oriented people who value community connection." This combination informs both the content and the visual style.

blocks.heading: Situation Analysis
blocks.html:
Before writing a design brief, conduct a situation analysis — a review of the current context that the design must address. This includes:

Current state: What exists now? What is working or not working about existing communications or products?

Competitor analysis: How do similar organisations present themselves? What can we learn from them?

Opportunities and challenges: What is the client trying to achieve, and what obstacles might the design need to overcome?

blocks.heading: Presentation Medium
blocks.html:
The presentation medium is where and how the final product will be delivered to the audience. This significantly affects design decisions:

Print (posters, brochures): requires 300 PPI resolution; uses CMYK colour mode; no interactivity.

Digital screen (websites, apps): uses 72–96 PPI; RGB colour mode; can include animation and interactivity.

Social media: each platform has specific image dimensions and aspect ratios (e.g. Instagram square 1080×1080 px, YouTube thumbnail 1280×720 px).

Projected presentation: needs high contrast and large text visible from a distance.

blocks.heading: Style Guide
blocks.html: A style guide (brand guide) documents the visual standards that must be applied consistently across all materials for a project or organisation. It ensures anyone working on the project produces consistent, on-brand outputs.

items.label: Logo
items.description: Rules for logo usage: correct versions (full colour, reversed, greyscale), minimum size, clear space around the logo, and what NOT to do with it.

items.label: Colour Palette
items.description: The official brand colours with exact hex codes (RGB), CMYK values, and Pantone references. Primary and secondary palettes defined.

items.label: Typography
items.description: The approved typefaces for headings, body text, and captions. Includes font sizes, weights, and line spacing specifications.

items.label: Image Style
items.description: Guidelines on the type, tone, and treatment of photography and illustrations. E.g. "bright, natural lighting; real people; no stock-photo clichés".

blocks.heading: Writing a Good Design Brief
blocks.html:
A design brief is a document that establishes the purpose, audience, constraints, and requirements of a project.

Understanding the client's needs and asking clarifying questions before starting work prevents costly changes later.

A signed-off design brief protects both the designer and the client — it defines what "done" looks like.

blocks.heading: Ask before you start
blocks.text: The most common cause of project failure is misunderstood requirements. Before touching any software, ask: What does success look like? Who will use this? Where will it be published? What are the file format and presentation medium requirements?
Planning tools turn a vague project into a structured schedule. Gantt charts, task lists, and milestones are how professionals keep complex projects on track.

blocks.title: What is a Gantt Chart? — Project Management in Under 5
blocks.caption: A fast, clear explanation of how Gantt charts work and why project managers rely on them.

blocks.heading: Gantt Charts
blocks.html:
A Gantt chart is a horizontal bar chart showing project tasks, their duration, and their sequence over a timeline.

Each bar represents a task. Overlapping bars show tasks that happen simultaneously. The chart makes it immediately obvious if the project is on schedule.

items.label: Gantt Chart
items.description: Visualises all tasks on a timeline. Shows durations, dependencies, and schedule at a glance.

items.label: Task List
items.description: Breaks the project into discrete, actionable steps that can be tracked and assigned.

items.label: Milestones
items.description: Significant checkpoints in the timeline — e.g. "design mockup approved by client".

items.label: Storyboard
items.description: A sequence of sketches showing the planned flow and visual narrative of a video, animation, or interactive product.

blocks.heading: Design Plan Documents
blocks.html: Before producing a final digital product, designers create planning documents that visualise and test their ideas on paper (or digitally) before committing significant production time.

items.label: Thumbnails
items.description: Small, rough sketches (typically hand-drawn) exploring many possible layout ideas quickly. Used early in the design process to generate and compare multiple concepts before committing to one direction. Fast to produce — the goal is quantity of ideas, not detail.

items.label: Storyboard
items.description: A sequence of frames showing the visual progression of a time-based product (video, animation, interactive media). Each frame shows what the viewer sees at each stage, with notes about camera angles, transitions, narration, or action.

items.label: Wireframe / Sketch
items.description: A low-fidelity layout drawing showing the structure and element placement of a design — without colour, detailed graphics, or final typography. Used for websites, apps, and printed layouts. Shows where elements will sit and how the page is organised.

items.label: 🖼️ Thumbnails & Wireframes
points: Used for static designs (posters, websites, logos)
Thumbnails: tiny rough sketches for initial idea generation
Wireframes: structural layout showing element placement
No colour or final graphics — focus is on layout
Allows multiple ideas to be compared quickly

items.label: 🎬 Storyboards
points: Used for time-based media (video, animation)
Sequential frames showing what viewers see over time
Includes notes on transitions, audio, and camera angles
Shared with clients to approve the plan before production
Saves time and money by catching problems early

blocks.heading: Build in buffer time
blocks.text: Always add 10–20% buffer to your estimated task durations. Client feedback takes longer than expected. Technical problems happen. A schedule with no slack will fail at the first obstacle.
Understanding the production process helps you allocate time and resources effectively. Evaluation closes the loop — it's how you know whether the finished product actually does its job.

items.label: 🔍 Pre-production
points: Research the topic and audience
Write the design brief
Create storyboards and wireframes
Gather assets (images, audio, footage)
Plan the schedule with a Gantt chart

items.label: 🎨 Production
points: Create the actual product
Design, code, record, edit
Follow the design brief throughout
Regular saves and version control
Check against criteria as you go

blocks.heading: Post-production & Delivery
blocks.html:
Post-production: Testing, reviewing against the design brief, gathering feedback from the target audience, and refining based on results.

Delivery: Final export in required formats, handover to client, and documentation of the production process.

blocks.heading: Evaluation
blocks.html:
Evaluation assesses whether the finished product meets the original design brief criteria.

A good evaluation is evidence-based: reference specific features of the product and explain how they meet or fall short of each criterion.

Include feedback from the target audience (survey results, user testing observations) as evidence.

Suggested improvements based on evaluation demonstrate critical thinking.

blocks.heading: Evidence-based evaluation
blocks.text: Don't write "the poster looks good." Write: "The heading uses 36pt bold Inter on a dark background, creating strong contrast that meets the brief's legibility requirement — confirmed by feedback from three test viewers who could read it from 2 metres."

blocks.heading: Evaluation Criteria
blocks.html: Evaluation criteria are the specific standards a product is judged against. They must be established in the design brief — before production begins — so evaluation is objective rather than subjective.

items.label: Usability
items.description: Can the target audience use the product effectively? Includes learnability (how quickly a new user can understand it) and intuitiveness (whether controls/navigation feel natural without instruction).

items.label: Readability
items.description: Is the text legible? Consider font size, typeface choice, line length, leading, and contrast between text and background. Test by having real users read it.

items.label: Consistency
items.description: Are colours, fonts, icons, and layout applied consistently throughout? Inconsistency undermines professionalism and makes products harder to use.

items.label: Layout
items.description: Is the design well-organised? Does the layout reflect the principles of design — balance, alignment, whitespace, and visual hierarchy?

items.label: Graphics & Content
items.description: Are images and graphics appropriate, high quality, and relevant to the purpose and audience? Is all written content accurate and relevant to the brief?

items.label: Cost & Time
items.description: Was the product delivered within the agreed budget and time constraints specified in the design brief?

items.definition: A document outlining the purpose, audience, and requirements of a project.
A bar chart showing project tasks, durations, and schedule over time.
A significant checkpoint in a project timeline.
An assessment of whether a product meets the design brief criteria.
A sequence of sketches showing the planned flow of a video or animation.
Small, rough sketches used early in the design process to quickly explore and compare layout ideas.
A structural layout drawing showing element placement without colour or final graphics.
A document defining the visual standards (logo, colours, typography, image style) for consistent design output.
Measurable audience characteristics: age, gender, income, education, location.
Audience values, attitudes, interests, and lifestyle — what motivates their decisions.
How effectively a target audience can use a product, including learnability and intuitiveness.

What does "iterative" mean in the context of a design and production process?
options: Completing each stage exactly once before moving to the next
Repeatedly refining a product through cycles of design, testing, and improvement
Working backwards from the final deadline to set milestones

options: Only the client who is paying for the project
Any person or group who is affected by or has an interest in the project outcome
The lead developer assigned to the project
The end user responsible for testing the product

question.text: Which type of testing involves real users evaluating a completed system against their actual needs?
A project milestone is best described as:
options: A task that takes longer than expected
A key point marking the completion of a significant phase or deliverable
A weekly update meeting with the client

options: To produce the near-final product for client sign-off

question.text: Explain why evaluation should be evidence-based when assessing a digital media product. (3 marks)
question.explanation: Evidence-based evaluation is necessary because it ensures judgements are grounded in observable, specific features of the product rather than subjective opinion. (1 mark) By referencing specific design decisions (e.g. "the heading uses 24pt Inter Bold which creates clear hierarchy as required by the brief"), the evaluation demonstrates a clear link between the product and the design criteria. (1 mark) Evidence-based evaluation also provides actionable information — if a criterion is not met, specific evidence points to what needs to be improved. (1 mark)

question.text: A student is creating a promotional video for a local café. Identify four elements that should be included in the design brief. (4 marks)
question.explanation: Any four of: Purpose (what the video needs to communicate/achieve, e.g. promote the café's new menu), target audience (who the video is aimed at, e.g. local residents aged 18–40), constraints (time, budget, technical requirements, e.g. must be under 90 seconds, must run on social media), deliverables (what must be produced, e.g. one 90-second MP4 video), visual style requirements (e.g. must match the café's existing branding), deadline (when the product must be completed). (1 mark each)

question.text: A student has completed a digital media project. Write a detailed evaluation of a hypothetical digital poster, assessing it against two design criteria. Include evidence and suggest one improvement. (8 marks)
question.explanation:
Criterion 1 — The poster must be legible and easy to read for the target audience (2 marks): The main heading uses 36pt bold sans-serif type in white against a dark background, providing strong contrast that meets the legibility criterion. Body text is set at 12pt, which is the minimum recommended size for print, meaning this criterion is met.

Criterion 2 — The poster must include the event details (date, time, venue) prominently (2 marks): The event date and time are displayed in a large call-out box in the centre of the poster, making them immediately visible. The venue address appears in smaller text at the bottom, which is less prominent and partially obscures the requirement to display it prominently.

Evidence reference (2 marks): Feedback from three test viewers confirmed they could read the heading from 2 metres away, supporting the legibility assessment. Two of three viewers needed to look carefully to find the venue address.

Suggested improvement (2 marks): The venue information should be repositioned to a more prominent location and increased in font size to at least 16pt so it is as easily visible as the date and time. A distinct colour or border could also be used to separate it from other elements.

topics.title: Design Concepts
topics.description: Elements and principles of visual design, typography, colour, and compositional techniques

blocks.text: Every visual design — from a website to a printed poster — is built from the same seven fundamental building blocks called the elements of design. Understanding these elements is the first step to analysing and creating effective digital media.

items.label: Line
items.description: A path between two points. Lines can be straight, curved, thick, thin, solid, or dashed. Lines guide the eye, divide space, and create structure.

items.label: Shape
items.description: A 2D area defined by a boundary. Geometric shapes (circles, squares, triangles) feel structured. Organic shapes (freeform, natural) feel fluid and dynamic.

items.label: Space
items.description: Positive space is the subject; negative space is the empty area around it. White space (breathing room) improves readability and draws focus to key elements.

items.label: Texture
items.description: The visual or tactile quality of a surface — rough, smooth, grainy, soft. In digital design, texture is implied through pattern or visual technique.

items.label: Colour
items.description: Defined by hue (the colour family), saturation (intensity), and tone (lightness/darkness). Colour evokes emotion, guides attention, and establishes brand identity.

items.label: 3D Form
items.description: Three-dimensional objects — sphere, cube, cylinder, cone. Form adds depth and realism, creating the illusion of volume and weight in 2D designs.

items.label: Tone
items.description: The degree of lightness or darkness in a design, distinct from colour. A black-and-white photo still has tone. Tone creates contrast, depth, and mood.

blocks.title: The Elements of Design Explained
blocks.caption: A visual breakdown of how line, shape, space, texture, colour, form, and tone work in real designs.

blocks.heading: Elements vs Principles
blocks.text: The elements of design are the WHAT — the raw materials (line, shape, colour, space, texture, 3D form, tone). The principles of design are the HOW — the rules for combining those materials effectively. You need both to analyse or create strong designs.

The six principles of design describe how designers combine the elements to create effective, professional compositions. Apply these principles consciously and your designs will communicate more powerfully.

items.label: Balance
items.description: Visual weight distributed across the design. Symmetrical balance is formal and stable — each side mirrors the other. Asymmetrical balance is dynamic and modern — heavier elements offset by position, colour, or size.

items.label: Emphasis & Contrast
items.description: Making one element command attention as the focal point. Achieved through contrast in size, colour, shape, or position. Proportion — the relative size of elements — reinforces what matters most.

items.label: Dominance
items.description: One element clearly stands out above all others. A dominant element gives the design a clear focal point and prevents the viewer's eye from wandering without direction.

items.label: Unity
items.description: Elements feel like they belong together as one cohesive whole. Achieved through proximity (grouping related items) and repetition (reusing colours, fonts, shapes consistently throughout).

items.label: Pattern
items.description: A repeating visual element in a predictable sequence. Pattern creates rhythm, texture, and visual interest. Deliberately breaking a pattern creates strong emphasis at that point.

items.label: Movement
items.description: The path the viewer's eye follows through the design. Created using lines, shapes, contrast, and layout to guide attention in a deliberate sequence from one element to the next.

blocks.heading: Elements and Principles Working Together
blocks.html:
The elements and principles are not separate — they interact constantly. Colour (element) creates contrast (principle). Line (element) creates movement (principle). Space (element) creates emphasis (principle) by drawing attention to what is nearby.

Strong designers think in both directions: "What elements should I use?" and "How will these principles shape the viewer's experience?"

blocks.heading: Using principles to critique designs
blocks.text: When evaluating any design, work through the principles: Is there a clear focal point (emphasis/dominance)? Does the layout feel stable (balance)? Are related elements grouped (unity/proximity)? Are visual elements repeated consistently? Does the eye have a clear path (movement)? This framework applies to any design — poster, website, or infographic.

items.definition: The even distribution of visual weight across a composition.
Mirror-image layout, giving a formal, stable appearance.
Different elements balanced by position, size, or colour rather than mirroring.
Drawing attention to a specific element, making it the focal point.
The element that stands out most prominently in a design.
The quality of elements feeling like they belong together as a cohesive whole.
Grouping related elements together to show they are connected.
Reusing visual elements (colours, fonts, shapes) to create consistency.

blocks.text: Typography is the art of arranging type. Poor font choices make great content hard to read. Good typography creates hierarchy, guides the eye, and sets the tone before a single word is consciously processed.

blocks.title: Beginning Graphic Design: Typography
blocks.caption: GCFGlobal's clear introduction to fonts, typefaces, and how typography shapes communication.

items.label: Serif
items.description: Small decorative strokes at the ends of letterforms. Examples: Times New Roman, Georgia, Garamond. Traditional and authoritative — widely used in print and formal documents.

items.label: Sans-serif
items.description: Clean letterforms with no decorative strokes. Examples: Arial, Helvetica, Open Sans. Modern and readable — preferred for screens and digital interfaces.

items.label: Script
items.description: Mimics cursive handwriting or calligraphy. Examples: Brush Script, Pacifico. Decorative and personal — used for titles and accents, rarely for body text.

items.label: Monospace
items.description: Every character takes exactly the same width. Examples: Courier, Consolas. Used in coding environments and to represent technical or typed text.

blocks.heading: Typographic Spacing
blocks.html:
Three spacing concepts control how readable and professional your type looks:

Kerning — the space between two specific letters. Auto-kerning handles most situations, but some letter pairs (like "AV" or "To") can look awkward and require manual adjustment.

Tracking — overall letter-spacing applied uniformly across a word, sentence, or text block. Tight tracking feels modern and compact; loose tracking feels elegant or airy.

Leading — the vertical spacing between lines of text (line height). Too tight and text feels cramped; too loose and the paragraph loses cohesion. A leading of 120–150% of the font size is usually comfortable for body text.

items.label: ↔️ Kerning & Tracking
points: Kerning: space adjusted between two specific letters
Tracking: letter-spacing applied uniformly across a block
Both affect horizontal readability
Poor kerning makes words look disconnected
Very tight tracking impairs readability at body sizes

items.label: ↕️ Leading
points: Vertical spacing between lines of text
Called "line-height" in CSS and web design
Too tight = text feels cramped and hard to scan
Too loose = paragraph feels disconnected
Aim for 120–150% of font size for body text

blocks.heading: Type Hierarchy
blocks.html:
Limit designs to two typeface families — one for headings, one for body text. Use size, weight (bold, regular, light), and style to create hierarchy rather than adding extra typefaces.

Hierarchy makes it clear what to read first: large bold heading → medium subheadings → smaller body text. This guides the reader's eye through the content naturally.

blocks.heading: The golden rule of typography
blocks.text: Limit yourself to two font families in any single design. Use weight (bold vs regular), size, and spacing to create hierarchy. Adding a third font almost always makes a design look cluttered and unprofessional.
Composition is how you arrange elements within a design space. Three key compositional rules guide effective layout in digital media and design work.

items.label: Rule of Thirds
items.description: Divide the design into 9 equal parts using 2 horizontal and 2 vertical lines. Place key subjects or focal points at the intersections ("power points"). This creates more dynamic, interesting compositions than centring everything.

items.label: Grid & Alignment
items.description: Use an underlying grid to align all elements consistently. Every element should align with something else on the page. Consistent alignment makes designs look intentional and professional; misalignment looks accidental.

items.label: Reading Gravity
items.description: In left-to-right cultures, viewers naturally scan from top-left to bottom-right (Z-pattern or F-pattern). Place the most important information at the natural entry points — top-left and along the first horizontal scan line.

blocks.heading: Applying the Rules
blocks.html:
Rule of Thirds: Rather than centring your subject, try placing it at one of the four intersection points of a rule-of-thirds grid. This draws the eye more naturally and creates visual tension and interest.

Grid & Alignment: Professional design software (Adobe InDesign, Figma, Canva) all include grid and guide tools. Designs built on a clear grid look authoritative and coherent.

Reading Gravity: Place your headline top-left — that is where eyes land first. Call-to-action buttons and key messages should fall on or near the natural reading path, not hidden in corners.

blocks.heading: Rules are guidelines, not laws
blocks.text: Expert designers break these rules deliberately to create effect — the rule of thirds can be broken to create stillness and symmetry; a centred layout can feel authoritative. But you must understand a rule before you can break it with intention. Learn the rules first.
Colour is one of the most powerful tools in a designer's toolkit. It communicates emotion, directs attention, and establishes brand identity before a single word is read.

blocks.title: Colour Theory for Beginners
blocks.caption: A practical guide to the colour wheel, colour harmonies, and how to choose colours that work together.

blocks.heading: The Colour Wheel & Harmonies
blocks.html:
The colour wheel organises hues into primary (red, yellow, blue), secondary, and tertiary colours.

Complementary colours sit opposite each other on the wheel — high contrast, eye-catching. Analogous colours sit adjacent — harmonious and calm.

Triadic colour schemes use three evenly spaced colours for vibrant, balanced designs.

items.label: Hue
items.description: The colour itself — red, blue, green. What we typically mean when we name a colour.

items.label: Saturation
items.description: The intensity or purity of a colour. Fully saturated = vivid. Desaturated = muted or grey.

items.label: Tone / Value
items.description: The lightness or darkness of a colour. Adding white creates a tint; adding black creates a shade. Tone is a key element of design.

blocks.heading: Digital vs Print colour modes
blocks.text: Digital designs use RGB — colours specified as hex codes (#RRGGBB). Print designs use CMYK. Always work in the correct colour mode for your output — an RGB design sent to print can produce unexpected, inaccurate colours.

items.definition: The arrangement of elements to show their order of importance.
Empty space around elements that improves readability and focus.
A set of fonts sharing common design features (e.g. Arial is a typeface; Arial Bold is a font).
A selected set of colours used consistently throughout a design.
The space between two specific letters in a word.
Overall letter-spacing applied uniformly across a block of text.
Vertical line spacing — the distance between lines of text.
A compositional guideline dividing a design into 9 equal sections, with key subjects placed at the intersections.
The tendency of viewers to scan a design from top-left to bottom-right.

options: Elements placed close together are perceived as related
All text should be centred on the page

question.text: Identify and explain two principles of design that should be applied when creating a digital flyer for a school event. (4 marks)
question.explanation:
Any two of the following, with explanation:

• Balance — Distribute visual weight evenly across the design so it does not feel lopsided or top-heavy. A symmetrically balanced flyer looks formal and stable; asymmetrical balance can look dynamic and modern. (2 marks)

• Emphasis/Contrast — Use contrast in colour, size, or position to make the most important information (e.g. the event name) the first thing a viewer sees. A bold heading against a simple background creates strong emphasis. (2 marks)

• Unity/Proximity — Group related information together (e.g. event time and venue in one block) and repeat the same colours and fonts throughout the flyer to create a cohesive, professional design. (2 marks)

• Movement — Use layout, lines, or contrast to guide the viewer's eye from the headline through to the event details and call-to-action (e.g. registration link). (2 marks)

question.text: Explain the difference between kerning, tracking, and leading in typography. (3 marks)
question.explanation:
Kerning is the space adjusted between two specific letters and is used when a particular letter pair looks awkward. (1 mark)

Tracking is the overall letter-spacing applied uniformly across an entire word or block of text — it controls how tight or airy the text looks as a whole. (1 mark)

Leading is the vertical spacing between lines of text (line height). Too tight and text feels cramped; too loose and the paragraph loses cohesion. (1 mark)

question.text: Explain the difference between a serif and a sans-serif font, and give one situation where each would be appropriate. (4 marks)
question.explanation:
Serif fonts have small decorative strokes at the ends of letterforms (e.g. Times New Roman). They are traditionally used for long-form body text in printed documents because the serifs help guide the eye across the line. (2 marks)

Sans-serif fonts have no such strokes (e.g. Arial, Inter). They are preferred for screen-based content such as websites because they render more cleanly at small sizes on digital displays. (2 marks)

question.text: A student is designing an infographic to explain climate change for a Year 10 audience. Describe how they could apply the principles of emphasis and unity to improve the effectiveness of their design, and explain one compositional rule they should follow. (8 marks)
question.explanation:
Emphasis (3 marks): The student should identify the single most important statistic or message and make it visually dominant — using a much larger font size, bold weight, or high-contrast colour against the background. The key message should be placed where the viewer's eye naturally lands first (top-left or along the primary reading path). Contrasting the focal element with simpler surrounding elements ensures it commands attention before anything else is read.

Unity (3 marks): Applying unity means the infographic's sections should feel like one cohesive whole rather than separate pieces. The student can achieve this through proximity — grouping related data and labels together with clear white space between groups — and repetition — reusing the same two or three colours and typefaces consistently throughout all sections. Consistent icons, borders, and layout patterns also reinforce unity.

Compositional rule (2 marks): The student should apply the rule of thirds — dividing the infographic into a 3×3 grid and placing key visual elements (charts, pull-out statistics) at the four intersection points rather than centring them. This creates a more dynamic, interesting layout and draws the viewer's eye across the design naturally. Alternatively, reading gravity should be considered — placing the headline or most alarming statistic at the top-left, knowing that audiences in left-to-right reading cultures will look there first.

topics.title: Impacts of Technology
topics.description: Social, ethical, legal, and environmental implications of digital technologies

blocks.text: Digital technologies have transformed how we connect, learn, and work — but they also create new risks and responsibilities. Being a good digital citizen means understanding both sides.

blocks.title: Digital Citizenship Explained
blocks.caption: What does it mean to be a responsible, ethical member of the online world?

blocks.heading: Social Impacts of Technology
blocks.html:
The digital divide refers to the gap between those who have access to technology and the internet and those who do not — due to geography, income, age, or disability.

Remote work and online learning have become mainstream, fundamentally changing social structures and how organisations operate.

Social networking takes many forms: social media platforms (Facebook, Instagram), forums (Reddit), professional networking (LinkedIn), gaming communities, live chat rooms, and virtual worlds. Each creates a different type of virtual community with its own norms and risks.

items.label: ✅ Benefits
points: Stay connected with friends and family across distances
Build virtual communities around shared interests
Access news and information instantly
Platform for education and creative expression
Businesses can reach customers directly

items.label: ⚠️ Risks
points: Cyberbullying and online harassment
Misinformation and fake news spread rapidly
Negative effects on mental health and self-image
Addictive design keeps users engaged longer than intended
Privacy risks from oversharing personal information

blocks.heading: Cyberbullying
blocks.html: Cyberbullying is the use of digital technology to repeatedly harm or harass people. It can occur on any platform and has serious mental health consequences for victims.

items.label: Exclusion
items.description: Deliberately leaving someone out of online groups, chats, or games to make them feel isolated.

items.label: Harassment
items.description: Sending repeated offensive, threatening, or hurtful messages to a target.

items.label: Outing
items.description: Sharing someone's private or embarrassing information publicly without their consent.

items.label: Cyberstalking
items.description: Repeatedly monitoring, following, or contacting someone online to intimidate or control them.

items.label: Fraping
items.description: Accessing someone's social media account and posting content as them to damage their reputation.

items.label: Flaming
items.description: Posting deliberately offensive, aggressive comments to provoke an angry reaction (a "flame war").

items.label: Impersonation
items.description: Creating a fake account pretending to be another person to damage their reputation or relationships.

items.label: Trickery
items.description: Tricking someone into sharing private information or images, then sharing that content publicly.

items.label: Doxing
items.description: Researching and publicly posting someone's private information (address, phone number) online.

items.label: Trolling
items.description: Posting inflammatory or off-topic content to provoke emotional reactions from others.

blocks.heading: Managing Cyberbullying
blocks.html: If you experience or witness cyberbullying, the following strategies can help:

items.label: 🛡️ Protect Yourself
points: Do not respond — responding often escalates the situation
Block and report the bully on the platform
Save evidence (screenshots) of the bullying
Review and tighten your privacy settings

items.label: 🤝 Seek Support
points: Report it to a trusted adult (parent, teacher, school counsellor)
Contact the eSafety Commissioner (Australia) for serious cases
Reach out to a friend for emotional support
Remember: it is not your fault

blocks.heading: Work-Life Balance & Technology
blocks.html:
Technology has blurred the boundary between work and personal time. Always-on communication tools (email, messaging apps) can make it difficult to disconnect from work.

In 2024, Australia introduced a Right to Disconnect law, giving employees the legal right to refuse contact outside working hours without facing consequences. This recognises that constant digital connectivity can harm health, relationships, and productivity.

Healthy work-life balance strategies include: setting "no screen" times, turning off work notifications after hours, and establishing dedicated workspaces separate from rest areas.

blocks.text: Ethics in technology asks: just because we can collect data or deploy AI — should we? Ethical issues don't always have clear legal answers, but they matter for how technology affects real people.

items.label: Privacy
items.description: Apps and websites collect vast amounts of personal data. Who owns that data and how is it used?

items.label: Surveillance
items.description: CCTV, facial recognition, and tracking apps balance security against privacy rights.

items.label: AI Bias
items.description: AI trained on unrepresentative data can produce unfair outcomes in hiring, lending, or law enforcement.

items.label: Digital Divide
items.description: Unequal access to technology creates and reinforces existing social inequalities.

blocks.heading: The problem with AI bias
blocks.text: If an AI hiring tool is trained on historical data where one gender dominated a role, it can learn to prefer that gender — embedding past discrimination into future decisions. Ethical AI requires diverse training data and human oversight.
Unlike ethical questions, legal issues have definitive answers — and breaking these laws has real consequences. Copyright, privacy law, and cybercrime laws apply to students as much as anyone.

blocks.title: Creative Commons Explained
blocks.caption: Understand the different Creative Commons licences and what they allow you to do with others' work.

blocks.heading: Copyright Act 1968 (Australia)
blocks.html:
The Copyright Act 1968 is the key Australian law protecting the rights of creators over their original works — including text, images, music, video, and software.

Copyright is automatic — it exists from the moment a work is created and does not need to be registered. In Australia, copyright generally lasts for the creator's lifetime plus 70 years.

Fair dealing allows limited use of copyrighted material without permission for specific purposes: research, criticism, news reporting, parody, and education.

Private use allows individuals to make personal copies of legally owned works (e.g. ripping a CD for personal listening), but this does not extend to sharing copies with others.

blocks.title: Moral Rights — Three Rights Every Creator Has

items.label: Right of Attribution
items.description: The creator has the right to be identified (credited) as the author of their work. Using someone's design, photo, or writing without crediting them violates this right — even if you have permission to use the work.

items.label: Right of Integrity
items.description: The creator has the right to object to derogatory treatment of their work — changes that distort, mutilate, or modify it in a way that damages their honour or reputation. For example, cropping an artwork and using it in an offensive context.

items.label: Right Against False Attribution
items.description: The creator has the right not to have a work falsely attributed to them — someone else cannot claim the creator made something they did not make, or attach the creator's name to a modified version they did not approve.

blocks.text: Copyright gives you economic rights — the right to be paid and to control who copies, sells, or distributes your work. Moral rights are personal rights — they protect your reputation and connection to your work. Unlike copyright, moral rights cannot be transferred or sold. Even if you sell the copyright in a photograph, you still retain the moral right to be credited as its creator.

items.label: Copyright
items.description: Protects original creative works (text, images, music, software). Automatic — no registration needed in Australia.

items.label: Patent
items.description: Protects inventions and technical innovations. Must be registered. Gives the inventor exclusive rights to make, use, or sell the invention for up to 20 years.

items.label: Trademark
items.description: Protects brand names, logos, and slogans. Must be registered. Gives exclusive rights to use the mark for commercial purposes.

items.label: Registered Design
items.description: Protects the visual appearance (shape, colour, pattern) of a product. Must be registered. Different from a patent, which covers function.

blocks.heading: APA 7th Edition Referencing
blocks.html:
When you use someone else's work in your own project — even just an image or a statistic — you must acknowledge the source. Failing to do so is plagiarism, which is both unethical and, in commercial contexts, potentially illegal.

APA 7th edition is a widely used referencing style in Australian schools and universities.

Website reference format: Author, A. A. (Year, Month Day). Title of page. Site Name. URL

Example: Smith, J. (2023, March 15). Cyberbullying statistics in Australia. eSafety Commissioner. https://www.esafety.gov.au

Image from a website: Creator, A. A. (Year). Title of image [Photograph]. Platform. URL

When no author is listed, move the title to the author position. When no date is available, use (n.d.) in place of the year.

items.label: Copyright Act 1968
items.description: Core Australian law protecting creators' rights. Covers fair dealing, moral rights, and automatic protection of original works.

items.label: Creative Commons
items.description: Licences that let creators specify how their work may be reused — some allow free use with attribution (CC BY), others restrict commercial use (CC BY-NC).

items.label: Privacy Act 1988
items.description: Australian law regulating how organisations collect, store, and use personal information.

items.label: Cybercrime Act 2001
items.description: Criminalises unauthorised access to computer systems, data theft, and distributing malware — serious criminal offences with significant penalties.

blocks.heading: Using Creative Commons content
blocks.text: When sourcing images or music for a project, look for Creative Commons licensed content on Unsplash, Pixabay, or Freesound. Always check the specific licence — CC BY requires attribution; CC BY-NC prohibits commercial use. Always provide an APA reference even for free-to-use content.
The environmental cost of technology is invisible to most users — but manufacturing, running, and disposing of digital devices has a significant impact on the planet.

items.label: E-waste
items.description: One of the fastest-growing waste streams globally. Discarded devices contain toxic materials that leach into soil and water.

items.label: Energy Consumption
items.description: Data centres powering cloud computing consume enormous amounts of electricity — some regions use as much power as entire countries.

items.label: Resource Extraction
items.description: Manufacturing devices requires mining rare earth metals, causing deforestation and soil contamination.

items.label: Sustainability Efforts
items.description: Tech companies are investing in renewable energy, recycling programs, and longer device lifespans to reduce impact.

blocks.heading: What you can do
blocks.text: Keep devices longer before replacing them. Use manufacturer trade-in programs. Recycle old devices at authorised e-waste collection points rather than general waste. These actions reduce both e-waste and the demand for new resource extraction.

blocks.title: Scenario Challenges — Impacts of Technology
blocks.scenario: Read the three scenarios below and answer each structured response question. These are the kinds of case-study questions that appear in AIT ATAR exams for 6–10 marks. Use evidence from the scenario and your knowledge of legal frameworks, ethical principles, and social impacts in your responses.

questions.question: Scenario A — Copyright dispute: A Year 11 student creates a promotional video for a local café and includes a popular song from Spotify as the background music. The café posts the video on their Instagram page, which has 12,000 followers. The record label that owns the song sends the café a cease-and-desist letter. Identify two legal issues raised by this situation and explain how each applies. (4 marks)

Scenario B — Privacy and data breach: A student runs a small tutoring business and collects clients' names, email addresses, phone numbers, and payment details in a Google Sheet shared with a single business partner. The partner accidentally makes the sheet publicly viewable for 48 hours. During this time, the personal details of 34 clients are accessible to anyone with the link. Identify two obligations the student has under the Australian Privacy Principles and explain whether this incident must be reported under the Notifiable Data Breaches scheme. (6 marks)

Scenario C — Digital divide and social impact: A government initiative proposes replacing all paper-based Centrelink welfare applications with an online-only system, requiring applicants to submit forms, upload documents, and track their claims digitally. Discuss two potential negative social impacts of this change, with specific reference to the digital divide. (4 marks)

items.definition: The gap between those with and without access to digital technologies.
An individual's right to control personal information about themselves.
Legal protection of a creator's rights over their original work.
A set of licences allowing creators to share work with specified permissions.
Discarded electrical and electronic devices.
