question.text: Explain one positive and one negative social impact of social media technology. (4 marks)
question.explanation:
Positive social impact: Social media allows people to maintain connections with friends and family regardless of distance, and enables individuals to connect with communities who share similar interests or experiences, reducing social isolation. (2 marks)

Negative social impact: Social media can contribute to cyberbullying, where individuals are harassed or targeted online. This can have serious mental health consequences for victims, including anxiety, depression, and withdrawal from school or social activities. (2 marks)

question.text: A business collects customer data including names, email addresses, and purchase history. Describe two ethical responsibilities the business has regarding this data. (4 marks)
question.explanation:
The business should only collect data that is necessary for its stated purpose (data minimisation) and must be transparent with customers about what data is collected and how it will be used. (2 marks)

The business must store the data securely to prevent unauthorised access or data breaches, and must not sell or share the data with third parties without the customer's consent. (2 marks)

question.text: A school is considering installing facial recognition software at its entrances for security purposes. Discuss the potential benefits and ethical concerns of implementing this technology. (8 marks)
question.explanation:
Benefits (4 marks): Facial recognition could improve school security by automatically identifying and logging who enters the building, making it easier to detect and respond to unauthorised visitors. It can reduce the workload of staff who would otherwise need to manually check identification. In an emergency, it could help verify who is on the premises. The technology could also be used to automate student attendance tracking, saving administrative time.

Ethical concerns (4 marks): Collecting and storing biometric data (facial recognition data) raises significant privacy concerns, particularly for minors who cannot meaningfully consent. There is a risk that this data could be misused, hacked, or accessed by unauthorised parties. Facial recognition systems have been shown to have higher error rates for certain ethnic groups, raising concerns about bias and fairness. Students and staff may feel their privacy is being invaded and experience discomfort from constant surveillance, affecting the school environment.

topics.title: Application Skills
topics.description: Practical skills using digital media applications for image, audio, and video

blocks.text: Image editing is a core digital media skill. Whether you're producing a logo, editing a photo, or creating a graphic for a website, understanding file formats and image types is essential.

blocks.title: Raster vs Vector — What's the Difference?
blocks.caption: Understand when to use pixels vs paths — and why choosing the wrong format causes problems.

items.label: 📷 Raster Images
points: Made up of individual pixels
Quality degrades when enlarged (becomes pixelated)
Best for photographs and complex colour images
Formats: BMP, GIF, JPEG/JPG, TIFF, PNG
Resolution-dependent — measured in PPI

items.label: ✏️ Vector Images
points: Made from mathematical paths (lines and curves)
Scale to any size without quality loss
Best for logos, icons, and illustrations
Formats: SVG, CGM, AI (Adobe Illustrator)
Resolution-independent — crisp at any size

items.label: BMP (Bitmap)
items.description: Uncompressed — very large file sizes. Stores every pixel without quality loss. Rarely used for web; sometimes used in Windows system graphics.

items.label: GIF
items.description: Limited to 256 colours. Supports simple animation and transparency. Best for short animated graphics and simple icons.

items.label: JPEG / JPG
items.description: Lossy compression — small file sizes with some quality reduction. Best for photographs. Does not support transparency. Standard for web photos.

items.label: TIFF
items.description: Supports lossless or lossy compression. High quality, large files. Industry standard for professional print and archival photography.

items.label: PNG
items.description: Lossless compression. Supports transparent backgrounds. Best for logos, graphics, and screenshots where quality matters.

items.label: SVG (Scalable Vector Graphic)
items.description: Open standard for web vector graphics. Crisp at any size, small file size for simple graphics. Widely used for logos and icons on websites.

items.label: CGM (Computer Graphics Metafile)
items.description: An older ISO standard vector format used in technical and engineering documents. Less common today but still used in some industries.

blocks.heading: Resolution rules
blocks.text: 72 PPI is standard for screen-based designs. 300 PPI is the minimum for print. Always create at the highest resolution you need — you can reduce quality later, but you cannot add detail that was never there.
Audio and video production skills are increasingly valuable. Understanding formats, codecs, resolution, and frame rate separates amateur work from professional output.

items.label: MP3
items.description: Lossy compression — small file size with some quality loss. Widely compatible with all devices and platforms. Best for distributing music and podcasts.

items.label: WAV
items.description: Uncompressed — maximum audio quality, very large file sizes. Used for recording and professional audio editing. Standard in studios and broadcast production.

items.label: WMA (Windows Media Audio)
items.description: Microsoft's proprietary compressed audio format. Smaller file sizes than MP3 at similar quality. Mainly used in Windows environments; limited cross-platform compatibility.

items.label: AAC
items.description: Compressed with better quality than MP3 at the same file size. Used by Apple and YouTube. Good balance of quality and compatibility.

items.label: 🎵 MP3 — Best For Distribution
points: Lossy compression — some quality lost permanently
Very small file sizes (3–5 MB per song)
Compatible with virtually every device
Standard for streaming and podcast distribution
Not ideal for editing (re-saving degrades quality further)

items.label: 🎼 WAV — Best For Production
points: Uncompressed — no quality loss
Very large file sizes (30–50 MB per song)
Industry standard for recording and editing
Preserves maximum quality for further processing
Not practical for web delivery or streaming

blocks.heading: Audio Editing Essentials
blocks.html:
Key audio tasks: trimming (cutting unwanted sections), fading in/out, normalising volume (ensuring consistent levels), noise reduction, and mixing multiple tracks.

Sample rate (Hz) determines audio quality. 44,100 Hz (44.1 kHz) is CD quality; 48,000 Hz is standard for video production.

items.label: 📺 Resolution
points: 1920×1080 — Full HD (standard for YouTube)
Higher resolution = larger file size
Match output to platform requirements

items.label: 🎬 Frame Rate (FPS)
points: 24 FPS — cinematic feel
30 FPS — broadcast standard
60 FPS — smooth motion, gaming
Higher FPS = larger file, smoother motion
Match the frame rate of your source footage

blocks.heading: Best export format for YouTube
blocks.text: MP4 with H.264 codec at 1920×1080, 30 FPS. This is YouTube's recommended format — good quality at manageable file sizes, and compatible with virtually every device.
Professional media production relies on disciplined file management. A disorganised project folder costs hours of time and risks losing irreplaceable work.

blocks.heading: File Organisation
blocks.html:
Organise files into clearly named folders using a logical hierarchy: /ProjectName/Assets/Images/, /Audio/, /Video/, /Exports/.

File naming conventions: use descriptive, consistent names. logo_final_v3.ai is far better than final_FINAL_USE_THIS.ai. Include dates in filenames for time-sensitive assets (e.g. report_2024-05-01.docx).

Metadata provides additional information about a file beyond its content — such as author, creation date, file size, and copyright information. Good metadata makes files searchable and helps prove ownership.

blocks.heading: Version Control
blocks.html:
Version control means saving incremental versions of a file (e.g. poster_v1.ai, poster_v2.ai, poster_v3_client_feedback.ai).

Never overwrite your only copy. Keep each significant version so you can return to an earlier state if a client rejects changes.

blocks.heading: Compression
blocks.html:
Compression reduces file size for storage or transmission. There are two types:

Lossless compression: reduces file size without discarding any data. The original can be perfectly reconstructed (e.g. PNG, ZIP, WAV).

Lossy compression: discards some data to achieve smaller file sizes. Quality is reduced but often imperceptible to the average viewer (e.g. JPEG, MP3).

blocks.text: Understanding how to install and maintain software — and the legal rules around using it — is an essential professional skill. All software comes with a licence that governs how it can be used.

blocks.heading: Installing Software
blocks.html:
Before installing software, always verify that your hardware meets the minimum system requirements (CPU, RAM, storage, OS version).

Download software only from official developer websites or trusted sources to avoid malware bundled with unofficial installers.

General installation steps: (1) Download the installer. (2) Run the installer and accept the End User Licence Agreement (EULA). (3) Choose installation location (default is usually correct). (4) Complete setup and restart if prompted. (5) Verify the software opens and runs correctly.

Software updates are critical for security. Updates patch known vulnerabilities that attackers can exploit. Enable automatic updates where possible, or check for updates regularly.

items.label: Closed Source (Proprietary)
items.description: Source code is hidden. Users pay for a licence to run the software but cannot copy, modify, or redistribute it. Licences are tied to a user or device.

items.label: Open Source
items.description: Source code is publicly available. Anyone can view, modify, and redistribute it. Usually free. Developed by communities or non-profits.

items.label: Shareware
items.description: Distributed free to try, but requires payment for full features or continued use. Trial versions often have time limits or locked features.

items.label: Freeware / Freemium
items.description: Freeware is permanently free with no paid tier. Freemium is free for core features with optional paid upgrades for advanced functionality.

apps.name: Microsoft 365
apps.note: Subscription-based Office suite (Word, Excel, PowerPoint). Each licence is per user — sharing or copying is prohibited.

apps.name: Adobe Photoshop
apps.note: Professional image editor sold via Creative Cloud subscription (~AUD $37/month). Source code is private.

apps.name: Windows 11
apps.note: Microsoft's operating system. EULA prohibits reverse engineering. Licence is typically included with device purchase.

apps.name: Mozilla Firefox
apps.note: Free web browser maintained by the non-profit Mozilla Foundation. Full source code is on GitHub — anyone can inspect or contribute.

apps.name: VLC Media Player
apps.note: Plays virtually any video or audio format. Developed by the VideoLAN community under the GNU GPL licence — free forever.

apps.name: LibreOffice
apps.note: Free, community-driven alternative to Microsoft Office. Used in schools and government agencies across Australia.

apps.name: WinRAR
apps.note: File compression tool with a 40-day free trial. After the trial, a "nag screen" asks you to purchase (USD $29) — but the software keeps working.

apps.name: WinZip
apps.note: Another popular compression utility with a 21-day free trial. After the trial, core features are paywalled — you must buy to continue.

apps.name: Malwarebytes
apps.note: Malware removal tool. The free version scans on-demand only; the paid Premium tier adds real-time protection and a 14-day free trial.

apps.name: Spotify
apps.note: Music streaming with a free ad-supported tier. Spotify Premium (~AUD $12/month) removes ads and enables downloads for offline listening.

apps.name: Canva
apps.note: Graphic design platform. The free tier covers most everyday needs; Canva Pro unlocks premium templates, brand kits, and background remover.

apps.name: Zoom
apps.note: Video conferencing. Free meetings are capped at 40 minutes with 100 participants. Paid plans remove the time limit and add cloud recording.

blocks.heading: Using software without a licence
blocks.text: Installing proprietary software without a valid licence (software piracy) is illegal under copyright law. It can also expose your system to security risks, as pirated software is often bundled with malware. Always use legally licensed software.
VLOOKUP and HLOOKUP are two of the most-tested spreadsheet functions in AIT ATAR. Master these two functions and you can pull data from any table in seconds — no manual searching required.

blocks.title: Excel VLOOKUP Tutorial — Everything You Need To Know
blocks.caption: Watch this before trying the exercises below. Pay close attention to the four arguments — you'll need to fill them in from memory.

args.name: lookup_value
args.description: The value you are searching for — usually a cell reference like B2. Excel looks for this value in the first column of your table.

args.name: table_array
args.description: The range of cells that contains your data table — e.g. A1:D50. The first column of this range is always where the search happens.

args.name: col_index_num
args.description: Which column number (within the table_array) to return data from. Column 1 = the search column; column 2 = next column right; and so on.

args.name: range_lookup
args.description: FALSE for exact match (almost always what you want). TRUE for approximate match — only valid when data is sorted in ascending order.

blocks.title: Build the VLOOKUP formula
blocks.instruction: A student ID is in cell B2. The student table runs from A1 to D50. You want the student's email address from the 3rd column. Use an exact match.
blocks.context: Table layout: Column A = Student ID, Column B = Name, Column C = Email, Column D = Score. You need to find the email for the ID stored in B2.

args.name: lookup_value
args.description: The value you are searching for — usually a cell reference like C5. Excel looks for this value across the first ROW of your table (not a column).

args.name: table_array
args.description: The range of cells containing your horizontal data table — e.g. B1:M5. The first ROW of this range is always searched.

args.name: row_index_num
args.description: Which row number (within the table_array) to return data from. Row 1 = the search row; row 2 = the row below; and so on.

args.name: range_lookup
args.description: FALSE for exact match (recommended). TRUE for approximate match — only use when the first row is sorted in ascending order.

blocks.title: Build the HLOOKUP formula
blocks.instruction: A month name is stored in cell C5. Your sales data table runs from B1 to M5 (months across the top). You want the sales figure from row 2. Use an exact match.
blocks.context: Table layout: Row 1 = Month names (Jan–Dec), Row 2 = Monthly sales figures. C5 holds the month name you are looking up.

blocks.title: VLOOKUP vs HLOOKUP — when to use each

items.label: ↕️ VLOOKUP
points: Data arranged in COLUMNS (most common layout)
Search value must be in the FIRST COLUMN of your table
You return data from a specified column number
Example: look up a Student ID in column A, return their grade from column D
Used in almost every business spreadsheet — data is usually vertical

items.label: ↔️ HLOOKUP
points: Data arranged in ROWS (less common layout)
Search value must be in the FIRST ROW of your table
You return data from a specified row number
Example: look up a month name in row 1, return the sales figure from row 3
Used when categories run across the top and values run downwards

blocks.heading: Try it yourself in Excel
blocks.text:
1. Open Excel. Create these columns: A=StudentID, B=Name, C=Class, D=Score. Add 5 rows of data.
2. In cell F2, type one of your Student IDs.
3. In cell G2, type: =VLOOKUP(F2, A1:D6, 2, FALSE)
4. Press Enter — Excel returns the matching Name.
5. Change the 2 to 4 to return the Score instead.
6. Now try HLOOKUP: rotate your table (months across row 1, values in row 2) and retrieve a monthly value.

blocks.heading: Sort & Filter
blocks.html:
Sort rearranges all rows based on one or more columns — alphabetically, numerically, or by date. Multi-level sort lets you sort by surname first, then by first name when surnames match.

Filter hides rows that do not meet your criteria without deleting them. Combine sort and filter to instantly find the top 10 scores, all entries from one category, or any subset of your data.

items.definition: Searches the first column of a table range for a value and returns data from a specified column in the same row.
Searches the first row of a table range for a value and returns data from a specified row below it.
The value being searched for in a VLOOKUP or HLOOKUP — usually a cell reference (e.g. B2).
The range of cells (the data table) that the lookup function searches through.
In VLOOKUP, the column number within the table_array from which to return a value.
In HLOOKUP, the row number within the table_array from which to return a value.
Setting range_lookup to FALSE in VLOOKUP/HLOOKUP forces the function to return a result only when an identical match is found.
An image made up of pixels; quality degrades when enlarged.
An image made from mathematical paths; scales without quality loss.
Software that compresses and decompresses video or audio data.
Compression that reduces file size without losing any data.
Compression that discards some data to achieve smaller file sizes.

question.text: Which image format supports transparent backgrounds?
What does "frame rate" refer to in video production?
options: The physical size of the video frame in pixels
The number of frames displayed per second
The compression ratio of the video file
The colour depth of the video

question.text: A student exports a logo for use on a website. Which format is most appropriate if the logo needs a transparent background?
In VLOOKUP, what does the third argument (col_index_num) specify?
options: The column number to search within
The number of the column in the lookup table from which to return a value
Whether the match should be exact or approximate
The range of cells to search across

question.text: What is the key difference between VLOOKUP and HLOOKUP?
options: VLOOKUP is faster; HLOOKUP is more accurate
VLOOKUP searches the first column of a range; HLOOKUP searches the first row
VLOOKUP works in Excel; HLOOKUP only works in Google Sheets
VLOOKUP returns text; HLOOKUP returns numbers

question.text: In a VLOOKUP formula, setting range_lookup to FALSE (or 0) means:
options: The function will find the closest match to the lookup value
The function will return FALSE if no match is found
The function requires an exact match to the lookup value
The lookup table must be sorted in ascending order

question.text: A spreadsheet has a product table in columns A–D (ProductID, Name, Category, Price). A student enters =VLOOKUP("P005",A2:D50,4,0) in another cell. What will this formula return?
options: The row number where P005 is found

question.text: When using the Sort feature in a spreadsheet, what must you do to avoid corrupting your data when sorting by one column?
options: Sort only the column you want sorted, not the whole table
Select the entire table so all rows move together
Sort from Z to A first, then A to Z
Remove any formula cells before sorting

question.text: A student is editing a photograph and wants to resize it without losing quality. Which image type is most suitable?
A video editor is choosing a frame rate for a cinematic short film. Which frame rate gives the most "film-like" look?
Explain the difference between raster and vector images and state one situation where each would be most appropriate. (4 marks)
question.explanation:
Raster images are composed of individual pixels and can display complex, photographic detail. However, they lose quality when enlarged. A raster image is most appropriate for photographs (e.g. JPEG for a portrait photograph). (2 marks)

Vector images are composed of mathematical paths and can be scaled to any size without losing quality. A vector image is most appropriate for logos and icons that must appear at multiple sizes without distortion (e.g. SVG or AI format). (2 marks)

question.text: A student is producing a video for YouTube. State and justify the resolution, frame rate, and file format they should choose for their final export. (6 marks)
question.explanation:
Resolution: 1920×1080 (Full HD) — this is the standard HD resolution recommended by YouTube, providing good visual quality while keeping file sizes manageable. (2 marks)

Frame rate: 30 FPS (frames per second) — this is the standard broadcast frame rate that gives smooth motion for general content and is widely supported by YouTube's platform. (2 marks)

File format: MP4 with H.264 codec — this is YouTube's recommended format as it provides good quality at relatively small file sizes, ensuring fast upload and smooth playback for viewers. (2 marks)

question.text: A student is creating a promotional package for a local business that includes: a logo, a brochure, and a promotional video. For each deliverable, identify the most appropriate file format and justify your choice. (9 marks)
question.explanation:
Logo (3 marks): SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) — the logo needs to be used at multiple sizes (on websites, business cards, signage) without any loss of quality. Vector format ensures crisp, clean edges at any scale. It can also be exported as PNG for web use when transparency is needed.

Brochure (3 marks): PDF (Portable Document Format) — PDFs preserve the layout, fonts, and images regardless of what device or operating system is used to view it. It is the industry standard for distributing designed documents for print and digital distribution, ensuring the design appears exactly as intended.

Promotional video (3 marks): MP4 with H.264 codec — this format provides a good balance between video quality and file size, is universally compatible with web platforms, social media, and most devices, and can be easily shared or uploaded to platforms like YouTube or Vimeo.

topics.title: Managing Data
topics.description: Data storage, database design, and information management for business

blocks.text: Businesses run on data. Understanding the difference between raw data and meaningful information — and how relational databases organise both — is foundational for Unit 2.

blocks.title: Relational Database Concepts
blocks.caption: A clear explanation of tables, keys, and relationships — the building blocks of every database.

items.label: 📊 Data
points: Raw, unprocessed facts and figures
No context — meaning is unclear
Examples: "27", "Perth", "Blue"
Collected by sensors, forms, systems
On its own, not useful for decisions

items.label: 💡 Information
points: Data that has been processed and given context
Meaningful and useful for decision-making
Example: "27 customers in Perth prefer Blue packaging"
Produced by analysing and organising data
Enables businesses to act and plan

blocks.heading: Relational Databases
blocks.html:
A relational database organises data into tables (relations). Each table has rows (records) and columns (fields/attributes).

A primary key is a unique identifier for each record — e.g. CustomerID. No two records can share the same primary key.

A foreign key is a field in one table that references the primary key of another, creating a relationship between tables.

Database Management Systems (DBMS) — such as Microsoft Access, MySQL, and PostgreSQL — create and manage databases.

blocks.heading: Primary key vs Foreign key
blocks.text: Think of a primary key as a unique ID badge — every record has exactly one. A foreign key is like a reference to someone else's ID badge — it links a record in one table to a record in another. Together, they create the "relational" part of a relational database.
A database is more than just tables of data. The different database objects — tables, queries, forms, and reports — each serve a distinct purpose in how data is stored, accessed, and presented.

items.label: Tables
items.description: Store the raw data in rows (records) and columns (fields). The foundation of every database.

items.label: Queries
items.description: Retrieve, filter, and sort data from one or more tables. Written in SQL (Structured Query Language).

items.label: Forms
items.description: Provide a user-friendly interface for entering and editing data without working directly in tables.

items.label: Reports
items.description: Present data in a formatted, printable layout — often summarising query results for stakeholders.

blocks.heading: SQL: Querying a Database
blocks.html:
SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language for working with relational databases. Even without writing code, understanding SQL helps you understand how databases retrieve information.

A SELECT query retrieves data: SELECT FirstName, Email FROM Customers WHERE City = 'Perth' — this returns the first name and email of every customer in Perth.

You can sort results with ORDER BY (e.g. ORDER BY LastName ASC), count records with COUNT(*), and combine tables using JOIN.

Queries can pull from multiple tables at once — for example, joining an Orders table to a Customers table using the shared CustomerID to see each customer's order history.

blocks.heading: Table Relationships
blocks.html:
Relationships link tables together using primary and foreign keys. The most common type is one-to-many: one customer can have many orders, but each order belongs to one customer.

Many-to-many relationships (e.g. students enrolled in many subjects, each subject having many students) are handled by creating a junction table with foreign keys from both sides.

Referential integrity ensures that a foreign key value must match an existing primary key — you cannot add an order for a customer that doesn't exist.

blocks.heading: SQL: the language of databases
blocks.text: SQL (Structured Query Language) is used to write queries. A basic SELECT query looks like: SELECT CustomerName, Email FROM Customers WHERE City = 'Perth'. This retrieves the name and email of every customer in Perth.
A database is only as good as the data inside it. Data integrity rules and validation checks ensure that what gets into the database is accurate, consistent, and actually useful.

blocks.heading: Data Integrity
blocks.html:
Data integrity ensures data is accurate, consistent, and reliable throughout its lifecycle.

Data redundancy occurs when the same data is stored in multiple places — leading to inconsistency when one copy is updated but another is not. Normalisation reduces redundancy by structuring tables to store each piece of data once.

items.label: Presence Check
items.description: Ensures a required field cannot be left empty. Example: an order must have a customer name.

items.label: Range Check
items.description: Ensures a value falls within acceptable bounds. Example: age must be between 0 and 120.

items.label: Format Check
items.description: Ensures data matches a required pattern. Example: date must be entered as DD/MM/YYYY.

items.label: Type Check
items.description: Ensures data is the correct type. Example: a phone number field should only accept numbers.

blocks.heading: Validation vs Verification
blocks.text: Validation checks that data is in the right format — it cannot check if it's the right value. A date of birth entered as 01/01/2000 passes a format check even if the person was actually born in 1995. Verification (double-entry or human checking) is needed to catch that kind of error.

items.definition: A unique identifier for each record in a database table.
A field that references the primary key of another table.
A request to retrieve, filter, or sort data from a database.
Checks that ensure data entered meets defined criteria.
The unnecessary duplication of data across a database.

blocks.text: Efficient file management is a core professional skill. Choosing the right resolution and colour mode for digital versus print, organising files with consistent naming conventions, and using cloud storage effectively all prevent costly errors.

items.label: 🖥️ Digital / Online
points: Colour mode: RGB (Red, Green, Blue)
Resolution: 72 PPI (pixels per inch)
Lower resolution = smaller file size = faster loading
Designed for screen display — monitors use RGB light
Common formats: JPEG, PNG, SVG, WebP

items.label: 🖨️ Print
points: Colour mode: CMYK (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key/Black)
Resolution: 300 PPI (pixels per inch)
Higher resolution = sharper printed output
Designed for physical ink on paper — printers use CMYK ink
Common formats: PDF, TIFF, EPS, high-res JPEG

blocks.heading: Reducing File Size
blocks.html:
Large files slow down websites, consume storage, and are slow to share. Two key techniques reduce file size without replacing the content:

Cropping: removing unnecessary parts of an image reduces the total number of pixels and therefore the file size. A photo with irrelevant background should be cropped to the key subject.

Resampling (downsampling): reduces the number of pixels in an image. A 4000×3000 px photo can be resampled to 800×600 px for web use — a fraction of the original file size with no visible quality loss on screen.

blocks.heading: Document Version Control
blocks.html:
Version control means systematically tracking changes to files so earlier versions can be recovered. Three common approaches:

Sequential numbering: filename_v1.docx → filename_v2.docx → filename_v3_final.docx. Simple and widely understood.

Date-based naming: report_2025-03-15.docx → report_2025-03-22_client_review.docx. Useful when the date a version was created matters more than a version number.

Automated tools: platforms like Google Docs, Microsoft 365, and GitHub automatically save every version with timestamps. Any previous version can be restored without manually saving copies.

items.label: Cloud Storage
items.description: Files stored on remote servers accessed via the internet. Examples: Google Drive, OneDrive, Dropbox. Files are accessible from any device; automatic backup prevents data loss.

items.label: Cloud Applications
items.description: Software that runs in a browser — no local installation required. Examples: Google Docs, Canva, Microsoft 365 Online. Always up-to-date; no manual updates needed.

items.label: Remote Access
items.description: Accessing a computer, network, or system from a different physical location via the internet. Examples: Remote Desktop, VPN. Enables flexible and remote working.

blocks.heading: Personal Data Organisation
blocks.html:
A logical folder structure and consistent naming conventions are the foundation of good data management. Without them, files become impossible to find and version control breaks down.

Folder hierarchy: organise files by project, then by type — e.g. /ProjectX/Images/, /ProjectX/Documents/, /ProjectX/Finals/. Avoid saving everything to the desktop or Downloads folder.

Naming conventions: use descriptive, consistent names with dates or version numbers. Example: ClientProposal_ABCCafe_v2_2025-04.docx rather than "document1.docx" or "finalfinalREAL.docx".

blocks.text: Not all information on the internet is reliable. Search engines help you locate information, but evaluating its credibility is a critical skill — especially when making business decisions or producing academic work.

blocks.heading: How Search Engines Work
blocks.html:
Search engines use three processes to deliver results:

Crawling: automated programs (bots or "spiders") continuously browse the web, following links to discover and read new pages.

Indexing: crawled content is stored and organised in a massive searchable database — the "index."

Ranking: when a search query is entered, algorithms rank indexed pages by relevance, quality, and authority to deliver the most useful results first.

items.label: AND
items.description: Both terms must appear. Narrows search results. Example: "climate change AND Australia" returns only pages discussing both topics together.

items.label: OR
items.description: Either term can appear. Broadens search results. Example: "cats OR dogs" returns pages about cats, dogs, or both.

items.label: NOT
items.description: Excludes pages with that term. Example: "football NOT rugby" returns football results but excludes any page mentioning rugby.

blocks.heading: Evaluating Data Source Reliability
blocks.html:
Before using information in a report or business decision, evaluate the source using three key criteria:

Currency: When was it published or last updated? Out-of-date information may be inaccurate, especially for technology statistics, legislation, or research data.

Author/Authority: Who wrote or published it? Government, academic, and established industry sources are more reliable than anonymous blogs or commercial promotions.

Purpose: Why was it created? Identify whether the source is informational, promotional, or opinion-based. Promotional content may be biased; research reports aim to be objective.

items.label: Disk Clean-Up
items.description: Removes temporary files, cached data, and other unnecessary files to free up storage space and improve system performance.

items.label: Disk Defragmentation
items.description: Reorganises fragmented files on a hard disk drive so they are stored in contiguous blocks, improving read/write speeds. Not needed for SSDs.

items.label: Anti-Malware
items.description: Scans and removes malicious software including viruses, spyware, and ransomware. Requires regular updates to detect new threats.

items.label: SPAM Protection
items.description: Filters unwanted or malicious email before it reaches the inbox, reducing exposure to phishing and keeping communications manageable.

items.definition: Red, Green, Blue — the colour mode used for digital screens and web images.
Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key (Black) — the colour mode used for print production.
Pixels per inch — a measure of image resolution. 72 PPI for screen; 300 PPI for print.
Changing the number of pixels in an image to reduce file size or change its dimensions.
Storing and accessing data and applications over the internet rather than on a local device.
Words (AND, OR, NOT) used to refine and control the scope of search queries.
The process by which search engine bots discover and read web pages to build a searchable index.
Systematically tracking file changes so earlier versions can be retrieved when needed.

question.text: What is a primary key in a database?
options: The most important field in a table
A unique identifier for each record in a table
A field that links two tables together
A query that retrieves all records
