What type of relationship exists between a "Students" table and a "Courses" table if each student can enrol in many courses and each course can have many students?
Which SQL clause is used to filter records in a query?
A "presence check" validation rule in a database ensures that:
options: The field only accepts numeric values
The entered value matches an entry in a related table
The field cannot be left blank
The value falls within a specified range

question.text: In database terminology, what is a "record"?
options: A single column of data in a table
A complete set of related fields for one entity, stored as a single row
A query that retrieves all data from a table
The name given to a table within the database

question.text: Which database object is used to display formatted summaries or totals from query results, suitable for printing?
What is the purpose of a foreign key in a relational database?
options: To uniquely identify each record in its own table
To enforce a link between two tables by referencing the primary key of another table
To prevent users from editing records in the table
To store encrypted copies of sensitive data

question.text: A student enters "abc" into a field that requires a date. Which validation type would catch this error?
Explain the difference between data and information, using an example related to a business context. (4 marks)
question.explanation:
Data refers to raw, unprocessed facts or figures that on their own have no meaning. For example, the number "47" is data — it could refer to anything. (2 marks)

Information is data that has been processed or placed in context so it becomes meaningful. For example, "47 customer complaints were received in March 2025" is information — it has context (what was measured, when) that allows a business to draw conclusions and make decisions. (2 marks)

question.text: A business stores customer orders in a database. Describe two validation rules that could be applied to an "Order Date" field to maintain data integrity. (4 marks)
question.explanation:
Presence check: A validation rule should ensure the Order Date field cannot be left empty. Every order record must have a date for the database to be useful and accurate. (2 marks)

Format check: The date must be entered in the correct format (e.g. DD/MM/YYYY). This ensures consistency in how dates are stored and prevents errors when sorting records by date or running date-based queries. (2 marks)

question.text: A small business currently stores all customer and order information in a single spreadsheet. The owner is considering switching to a relational database. Discuss two advantages and one disadvantage of making this change. (6 marks)
question.explanation:
Advantage 1 — Reduced data redundancy (2 marks): In a spreadsheet, customer details (name, address, phone) are repeated in every row for every order. A relational database separates customers and orders into two linked tables, so customer data is stored once and linked by a CustomerID. This reduces storage requirements and the risk of inconsistent data.

Advantage 2 — Improved data querying (2 marks): A relational database allows complex queries across multiple tables, enabling the owner to quickly extract meaningful information such as "all orders over $500 placed this month" or "customers who have not placed an order in 6 months." Spreadsheets can do some of this with formulas but are less powerful and harder to maintain as data grows.

Disadvantage — Cost and complexity of setup (2 marks): Setting up a relational database requires technical knowledge or hiring someone with database skills. The owner must design the table structure, relationships, and forms correctly before entering data. There is also a cost for DBMS software and time required for staff training, which may not be justified for a small business with limited data.

topics.title: Networks
topics.description: Network types, components, configuration, and communication protocols

blocks.text: Networks let computers communicate and share resources. From the Wi-Fi in this classroom to the global internet — it's all networks. Understanding the different types is the starting point.

blocks.title: Computer Networks: LANs and WANs Explained
blocks.caption: A clear walkthrough of how local and wide area networks work and how they connect.

items.label: LAN
items.description: Local Area Network. Connects devices within a small area — home, office, or school. Usually owned by one organisation.

items.label: WAN
items.description: Wide Area Network. Connects devices across cities or countries. The internet is the world's largest WAN.

items.label: WLAN
items.description: Wireless LAN. A LAN using Wi-Fi instead of cables. Every school Wi-Fi network is a WLAN.

items.label: MAN
items.description: Metropolitan Area Network. Covers a city or campus — larger than a LAN, smaller than a WAN.

items.label: ✅ Advantages
points: Shared access to files, printers, and internet
Easier communication — email, messaging, video calls
Centralised data storage and backup
Centralised security management and software updates
Enables remote work and flexible access

items.label: ⚠️ Disadvantages
points: If the network fails, all connected users are affected
Security risks increase as more devices are connected
Setup and maintenance require technical expertise
Malware can spread rapidly across a network
Higher initial cost than standalone computers

blocks.heading: How they connect
blocks.text: Your home network is a LAN. Your router connects that LAN to your ISP's WAN. The internet is a WAN of WANs — billions of devices connected through a global infrastructure of cables, satellites, and routers.
Networks don't run on software alone — physical hardware devices move data between devices, connect networks, and extend wireless coverage. Knowing what each device does is essential.

items.label: Router
items.description: Connects different networks and routes data between them. Your home router connects the LAN to the internet.

items.label: Switch
items.description: Connects devices within a LAN. Forwards data only to the intended device using MAC addresses — much more efficient than a hub.

items.label: Hub
items.description: Older device that broadcasts data to all connected devices. Inefficient and largely replaced by switches.

items.label: NIC
items.description: Network Interface Card. Hardware in every device that enables network connection via Ethernet or Wi-Fi.

items.label: Access Point
items.description: Extends a wired network wirelessly, allowing Wi-Fi devices to connect to the LAN.

items.label: Modem
items.description: Converts digital data to the signal format used by your internet connection (cable, fibre, phone line).

items.label: ✅ Switch (Modern)
points: Sends data only to the intended device
Uses MAC addresses for targeting
Much more efficient use of bandwidth
Reduces unnecessary network traffic
Standard in all modern networks

items.label: ❌ Hub (Legacy)
points: Broadcasts data to ALL connected devices
Every device sees every packet
Creates unnecessary traffic and collisions
Security risk — any device can see all data
Rarely used in modern networks

blocks.heading: Servers in a Network
blocks.html:
A server is a computer dedicated to providing services to other devices (clients) on the network. Common types:

File server: stores and manages shared files. All users on the network can save to and retrieve files from one central location.

Web server: hosts websites and delivers web pages to clients via HTTP/HTTPS.

Print server: manages access to shared network printers — users send print jobs to the server, which queues and directs them.

Mail server: sends, receives, and stores email for all accounts on the domain.

blocks.heading: Personal Wireless Network Setup
blocks.html:
A home or small office wireless network typically requires three components working together:

NIC (Network Interface Card): hardware built into every device (laptop, desktop, phone) that enables it to connect to a network — either via Ethernet cable or Wi-Fi.

Router: connects the local network to the internet via the ISP. Assigns IP addresses to devices via DHCP and manages traffic between the LAN and WAN.

Access Point (AP): extends wireless coverage. In a home the router often has a built-in AP. Larger buildings use separate APs connected back to the router to ensure full coverage throughout the space.

blocks.heading: Hardware Selection Considerations
blocks.html:
When choosing network hardware, three key factors apply:

Compatibility: hardware must support the same standards. A router must support the same Wi-Fi standard (Wi-Fi 5 / 6) as the devices connecting to it, or those devices will operate at a slower speed.

Usability: consumer-grade hardware (e.g. home routers) is designed for self-configuration via simple web interfaces. Enterprise hardware offers more control but requires technical knowledge to configure and manage.

ISP requirements: your internet service provider specifies the connection type (NBN fibre, ADSL, cable) and may supply or require a specific modem. Any additional hardware — such as a separate router — must be compatible with that connection.

blocks.text: Network topology describes the physical or logical layout of how devices are connected. The topology you choose affects performance, reliability, and cost.

items.label: Star
items.description: All devices connect to a central switch. Most common in modern LANs. Easy to manage; single switch failure takes down the whole network.

items.label: Bus
items.description: All devices share one backbone cable. Simple and cheap but a single cable break brings down the entire network. Largely obsolete.

items.label: Ring
items.description: Each device connects to exactly two others, forming a loop. Data travels in one direction. A single break can disrupt the whole network unless dual-ring is used.

items.label: Mesh
items.description: Every device connects to every other device. Highly fault-tolerant — multiple paths exist for every connection. Expensive to cable; used in critical infrastructure.

items.label: 🔌 Physical Topology
points: The actual physical layout of cables and devices
How devices are physically connected in a building
Determines cabling cost and difficulty
What you would see if you looked at the network room
Example: cables running from desks to a patch panel

items.label: 💡 Logical Topology
points: How data actually flows through the network
May differ significantly from the physical layout
Determined by protocols and switching behaviour
What you would see in a network diagram showing data paths
Example: a physical star can behave logically like a bus

blocks.heading: Why star is the standard
blocks.text: Star topology dominates because fault isolation is easy — if one cable or device fails, only that connection is affected. Adding new devices just means plugging into the switch. For businesses, the reliability trade-off is worth the cost of a central switch.

Data travels across networks through different physical media — cables, fibre, or wireless signals. The medium chosen affects speed, reliability, and cost. How devices are organised (architecture) determines how resources and control are distributed.

items.label: Optical Fibre
items.description: Transmits data as pulses of light through glass or plastic fibres. Extremely fast (Gbps), immune to electrical interference, and can span very long distances. Most expensive to install.

items.label: Copper / Ethernet Cable
items.description: Transmits data as electrical signals through twisted copper wire pairs (Cat5e, Cat6). Reliable and cost-effective for LANs. Subject to interference over long distances; maximum ~100 m per segment.

items.label: Wireless (Wi-Fi / Radio)
items.description: Transmits data as radio waves through the air. Convenient and mobile — no cabling required. More susceptible to interference, signal degradation through walls, and security risks than wired media.

blocks.heading: Transmission Rates
blocks.html:
Transmission rate (bandwidth) measures how much data can be sent per second. It is expressed in bits per second (bps) and multiples:

1 Kbps = 1,000 bps (kilobits per second)

1 Mbps = 1,000,000 bps (megabits per second)

1 Gbps = 1,000,000,000 bps (gigabits per second)

A typical Australian NBN home connection might be 25–100 Mbps. A business fibre connection might be 1 Gbps or more. Factors affecting actual speeds include: media type (fibre > copper > wireless), network congestion, distance from the exchange, hardware quality, and the ISP plan speed limit.

items.label: 🖥️ Client-Server
points: One or more dedicated servers provide services to clients
Centralised control — easier to manage and secure
User accounts and permissions managed from the server
More expensive due to dedicated server hardware
Used in schools, businesses, and enterprise environments

items.label: 🤝 Peer-to-Peer (P2P)
points: Each device can act as both client and server
Resources shared directly between devices
No dedicated server — cheaper and simpler to set up
Harder to manage and secure as the network grows
Used in small home networks and some file-sharing applications

blocks.text: Protocols are the rules that make communication possible. Without agreed protocols, devices from different manufacturers could not communicate — like trying to have a conversation in different languages.

items.label: TCP/IP
items.description: The foundational protocol suite of the internet. TCP ensures reliable delivery; IP handles addressing and routing.

items.label: HTTP / HTTPS
items.description: Transfers web pages. HTTPS encrypts the connection using TLS, protecting data in transit.

items.label: FTP
items.description: File Transfer Protocol — used to transfer files between computers on a network.

items.label: DNS
items.description: Domain Name System — translates domain names (google.com) into IP addresses devices can use.

blocks.heading: IP Addressing
blocks.html:
An IP address is a unique address that identifies a device on a network — like a postal address for data.

IPv4 addresses look like 192.168.1.1 (four numbers, each 0–255). IPv6 uses a longer format to accommodate more devices.

Your router assigns IP addresses to devices on your LAN using DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol).

items.definition: Local Area Network — a network connecting devices within a small geographic area.
A device that connects different networks and routes data between them.
A device that connects devices within a LAN and forwards data to the intended device.
A set of rules governing how devices communicate on a network.
A unique numerical identifier assigned to each device on a network.

question.text: Which network device connects different networks together and routes data between them?
A school has all computers connected to a central switch. This is an example of which network topology?
Which protocol is used to securely transfer web pages over the internet?
What is the main purpose of the Domain Name System (DNS)?
options: To assign IP addresses to devices on a network
To encrypt data transmitted between web servers and browsers
To translate human-readable domain names into IP addresses
To route data packets between different networks

question.text: In a mesh topology, what is the key advantage?
options: It uses the least amount of cabling of any topology
All devices are connected to one central switch for easy management
Multiple redundant paths exist, so the network continues if one connection fails
Data always travels in a single direction around the network

question.text: Which IP address class is reserved for private (internal) networks, such as a home or office LAN?
What does the acronym LAN stand for?
Which network device operates at the data link layer and uses MAC addresses to forward data only to the correct destination port?
A school uses both wired and wireless connections for its computers and tablets. What term describes this combined network type?
Which protocol is primarily responsible for breaking data into packets and reassembling them at the destination?
Explain the difference between a router and a switch in a business network. (4 marks)
question.explanation:
A router connects different networks together and is responsible for directing (routing) data packets between networks. In a business context, the router connects the internal LAN to the external internet (WAN) and assigns IP addresses to devices via DHCP. (2 marks)

A switch operates within a single LAN and connects multiple devices (computers, printers, servers). It uses MAC addresses to forward data only to the specific device it is intended for, making it more efficient than a hub. A switch does not connect to external networks. (2 marks)

question.text: A small business is setting up a new office network. Compare a star topology with a bus topology and recommend which one the business should use. (6 marks)
question.explanation:
Star topology: All devices connect to a central switch. If one device fails, the rest of the network is unaffected. Troubleshooting is easier as you can isolate individual connections. Adding new devices is straightforward. The main weakness is that if the central switch fails, the entire network is affected. (2 marks)

Bus topology: All devices share a single cable (backbone). It is simple and inexpensive to set up with few devices. However, if the backbone cable fails, the entire network fails. Network performance decreases as more devices are added because all data travels on the same cable. (2 marks)

Recommendation: The business should use a star topology. (1 mark) For a business environment, reliability and ease of management are critical. Star topology's fault tolerance (a single device failure does not bring down the network) and scalability (easy to add devices) make it far more suitable than a bus topology for a commercial setting. (1 mark)

question.text: A business is moving to a new office building and needs to design a network for 20 employees. They require internet access, shared file storage, and a wireless network for mobile devices. Describe a suitable network design, identifying the hardware required and justifying your choices. (8 marks)
question.explanation:
Network design overview (2 marks): A star topology LAN would be used for the 20 wired desktop computers, connecting to a central managed switch. A router would connect the LAN to the internet via the building's ISP connection. Wireless access points would be installed throughout the office to provide Wi-Fi for laptops, tablets, and smartphones.

Hardware identification and justification (6 marks):

Router (1 mark): Required to connect the office LAN to the internet (WAN). It will also act as a DHCP server, assigning IP addresses to devices automatically.

Managed switch with sufficient ports (e.g. 24-port) (1 mark): Connects all 20 wired computers and servers within the LAN. A managed switch allows network administrators to configure VLANs and monitor traffic, improving security and performance.

Wireless access points (2 marks): Multiple APs distributed throughout the office ensure full wireless coverage. Using a centralised wireless controller allows all APs to be managed from one interface and provides seamless roaming for mobile users. Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax) standard recommended for speed and capacity.

File server or NAS device (2 marks): A dedicated file server or Network Attached Storage (NAS) device provides centralised file storage accessible to all employees. This allows files to be backed up automatically, shared across the organisation, and secured with access permissions.

topics.title: Impacts of Technology
topics.description: Business impacts, cybersecurity, legal obligations, and digital compliance

blocks.text: Cybersecurity threats cost Australian businesses billions each year. Understanding how attacks work is the first step to defending against them — you can't protect against threats you don't understand.

blocks.title: Phishing Explained in 6 Minutes
blocks.caption: See exactly how phishing attacks work — and why even smart people fall for them.

items.label: Malware
items.description: Malicious software: viruses, worms, trojans, spyware. Designed to damage, disrupt, or steal.

items.label: Phishing
items.description: Deceptive emails or fake websites that trick users into revealing passwords or financial details.

items.label: Ransomware
items.description: Encrypts a victim's files and demands payment to restore access. Devastating for businesses.

items.label: Social Engineering
items.description: Exploits human psychology — not code. Attackers manipulate people into bypassing security.

blocks.heading: The human is the weakest link
blocks.text: Most successful cyberattacks don't exploit software vulnerabilities — they exploit people. A convincing phishing email, an urgent phone call from "IT support," or a USB drive left in a car park can bypass millions of dollars of security technology.

blocks.title: Spot the Phish
blocks.instruction: Examine each email carefully. Decide whether it is a legitimate message or a phishing attempt — then reveal the analysis to see how you did.

emails.subject: URGENT: Your account has been suspended — verify now
emails.body:
Dear Valued Customer,

We have detected suspicious activity on your PayPal account. Your account has been temporarily suspended for your protection.

You must verify your identity within 24 hours or your account will be permanently closed.

Click below to verify your identity immediately.

redFlags.detail: "paypa1-support.com" — uses the number 1 instead of the letter l to impersonate PayPal.
"URGENT", "24 hours", "permanently closed" — artificial pressure designed to prevent you from thinking carefully.
3:14 AM — legitimate automated alerts from companies are usually sent at normal times; attackers operate in different time zones.
"paypa1-secure-verify.net" is not PayPal's domain. Always hover before clicking.
"Dear Valued Customer" — PayPal always addresses you by your registered name.

emails.subject: Your GitHub password was recently changed
emails.body:
Hi mrgibbsteach,

This is a notification that the password for your GitHub account was changed on 21 May 2026 at 10:28 AM AWST.

If you made this change, no action is required.

If you did not make this change, please secure your account immediately by visiting github.com/settings/security.

emails.subject: Action required: Reset your school password before Friday
emails.body:
Hello Student,

Your school network password is about to expire. To avoid losing access to the student portal and your files, please reset your password using the link below before this Friday.

If you do not reset your password, your account will be locked and you will need to visit the IT office in person.

Please do not reply to this email.

redFlags.detail: "school-helpdesk-portal.com" is not your school's actual domain. IT departments use the school's own domain (e.g. school.wa.edu.au).
"Hello Student" — your school's IT system knows your name. Legitimate messages address you by name.
The reset link points to an external site, not your school's official system.
"Account will be locked" — designed to pressure you into acting without verifying the email first.
Legitimate password resets are triggered by you (by visiting IT's site) — they don't email you a direct link out of nowhere.

emails.subject: Your order #205-7391847-5829164 has been shipped
emails.body:
Hi Clayton,

Great news — your order has been shipped!

Order #205-7391847-5829164
Item: USB-C Hub 7-in-1
Estimated delivery: Thursday 23 May 2026

Track your package at amazon.com.au/orders.

Thank you for shopping with Amazon.

emails.subject: 🎉 Congratulations! You've been selected — claim your $500 gift card
emails.body:
CONGRATULATIONS!

You have been randomly selected from over 50,000 participants to receive a $500 Coles gift card as part of our National Customer Appreciation Campaign.

You must claim your prize within 48 hours. A small $4.95 processing fee is required to release your card.

Click below to claim your reward now!

redFlags.detail: You didn't enter a competition — unsolicited prize offers are nearly always scams.
"competitions-australia-official.net" is not affiliated with any legitimate competition organiser.
Legitimate prizes never require a fee to be paid before receiving the reward — this is the classic "advance-fee" scam pattern.
Legitimate businesses send promotional emails during business hours.
The 48-hour deadline and emoji in the subject line are high-pressure tactics designed to make you act without thinking.

blocks.text: Defending against threats requires multiple layers of protection — often called "defence in depth." No single tool is enough; effective cybersecurity combines technology, processes, and people.

items.label: Firewall
items.description: Monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic based on security rules. The first line of defence.

items.label: Antivirus
items.description: Detects and removes known malware. Must be kept updated to recognise new threats.

items.label: Encryption
items.description: Converts data into an unreadable format. Even if intercepted, encrypted data is useless without the key.

items.label: MFA
items.description: Multi-factor authentication requires two or more forms of verification — e.g. password + SMS code.

items.label: Software Updates
items.description: Patch security vulnerabilities before attackers can exploit them. Enable automatic updates.

items.label: Staff Training
items.description: Reduces risk from phishing and social engineering. Humans must understand the threats they face.

blocks.heading: Backup Strategies
blocks.html:
Backups are the last line of defence against ransomware, hardware failure, and accidental deletion. Without a working backup, recovering from a major incident may be impossible.

The 3-2-1 backup rule: keep at least 3 copies of data, on 2 different types of media, with 1 copy stored off-site (e.g. cloud or a different physical location).

Backups must be tested regularly — an untested backup is not a backup. Many organisations discover their backups are corrupted or incomplete only when they need them.

Recovery Time Objective (RTO) defines how quickly systems must be restored. Recovery Point Objective (RPO) defines how much data loss is acceptable (e.g. backups every 24 hours means up to 24 hours of data could be lost).

blocks.heading: Access Control & Password Policies
blocks.html:
The principle of least privilege means users should only have access to the systems and data they need to do their job — nothing more.

Strong password policies require: minimum 12 characters, a mix of upper/lowercase, numbers and symbols, no common words, and no reuse of previous passwords.

Role-based access control (RBAC) assigns permissions to roles (e.g. Manager, Staff, Read-only) rather than to individual users, making it easier to manage permissions across an organisation.

blocks.heading: Enable MFA everywhere
blocks.text: Multi-factor authentication is one of the single most effective security measures available. Even if a password is stolen, MFA prevents an attacker from using it. Enable it on every account that supports it — email, banking, school systems.
Beyond defending against attacks, businesses have legal obligations around how they handle data — and a duty of care for employees using technology in the workplace.

blocks.heading: Privacy Act 1988 & the Australian Privacy Principles
blocks.html:
The Privacy Act 1988 is the primary Australian law governing the handling of personal information by organisations. It establishes 13 Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) that set out how personal information must be managed.

The Act applies to all Australian Government agencies and most private sector organisations with an annual turnover above $3 million, as well as health service providers regardless of size.

items.label: APP 1 — Transparency
items.description: Organisations must have a clearly expressed, up-to-date privacy policy that is freely available. Individuals must be able to find out what information is held about them.

items.label: APP 3 — Collection
items.description: Organisations may only collect personal information that is reasonably necessary for their functions. Collection must be by lawful and fair means.

items.label: APP 5 — Notification
items.description: Organisations must notify individuals at the time of collection why the information is being collected, who it may be shared with, and how to access or correct it.

items.label: APP 6 — Use & Disclosure
items.description: Personal information may only be used or disclosed for the primary purpose it was collected. Secondary uses require consent or must fit a permitted exception.

items.label: APP 10 — Quality
items.description: Organisations must take reasonable steps to ensure personal information is accurate, up-to-date, and complete before using or disclosing it.

items.label: APP 11 — Security
items.description: Organisations must protect personal information from misuse, interference, loss, and unauthorised access, modification, or disclosure. Data no longer needed must be destroyed or de-identified.

blocks.heading: APP 12 & 13 — Access and Correction
blocks.text: Individuals have the right to access the personal information an organisation holds about them (APP 12) and to request corrections to information that is inaccurate, out-of-date, or misleading (APP 13). Organisations must respond to access requests within 30 days.

blocks.heading: Other Legal Obligations
blocks.html:
Workplace surveillance laws regulate whether employers can monitor employee communications and computer use — employees must generally be informed before monitoring occurs.

Software licensing agreements specify the terms under which software may be used. Using software without a valid licence is illegal under copyright law.

blocks.heading: The Notifiable Data Breaches (NDB) Scheme
blocks.text: When a data breach is likely to cause serious harm, Australian organisations must notify both the affected individuals AND the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC) as soon as practicable. Failure to notify when required can result in significant fines.

blocks.heading: Ergonomics & Workplace Health
blocks.html:
Ergonomics is the design of workplaces and tools to fit the capabilities and limitations of workers.

Poor ergonomics can cause repetitive strain injuries (RSI), eye strain, and chronic back problems.

Safe Work Australia guidelines: monitor at eye level, chair supporting the lower back, keyboard allowing elbows at 90°, regular screen breaks (20–20–20 rule: every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds).

blocks.title: Scenario Challenges — Cybersecurity & Legal Obligations
blocks.scenario: These scenarios are modelled on the extended-response questions in the AIT ATAR exam. Each scenario requires you to apply your knowledge of cybersecurity threats, security measures, and legal obligations to a realistic business situation. Aim for specific, evidence-based responses.

questions.question: Scenario A — Ransomware attack: A medium-sized accounting firm has been hit by a ransomware attack. All client files (tax returns, financial statements, personal details of 800 clients) have been encrypted. The attackers demand $50,000 in cryptocurrency within 48 hours or the data will be published online. The firm has a 3-day-old backup on an external drive stored in the office. Outline the steps the firm should take in the 24 hours following discovery of the attack, and explain whether the firm has any legal obligations to report this incident. (8 marks)

Scenario B — Social engineering attack: An employee at a hospital receives a phone call from someone claiming to be from "Microsoft IT Support." The caller says the employee's computer has been flagged for suspicious activity and asks them to install a "security tool" by visiting a link. The employee follows the instructions, granting the caller remote access to their workstation. The workstation has access to patient medical records. Identify the type of attack, explain why it was effective, and recommend three specific cybersecurity measures the hospital should implement to prevent this type of attack in future. (6 marks)

items.definition: Malicious software designed to damage or gain unauthorised access to systems.
A deceptive attempt to obtain sensitive information by impersonating a trusted entity.
Converting data into an unreadable format that requires a key to decode.
Multi-factor authentication — requiring two or more forms of identity verification.
Notifiable Data Breaches scheme — requires organisations to report serious data breaches.

blocks.text: Technology has transformed how businesses operate and how people communicate — but not everyone benefits equally. The digital divide describes unequal access to technology, while e-commerce has opened entirely new ways for businesses to reach customers.

blocks.title: The Four Types of Digital Divide

items.label: Access Divide
items.description: The gap between those who have physical access to internet and technology and those who do not. Rural and remote communities often lack reliable broadband infrastructure.

items.label: Use Divide
items.description: The gap between those who regularly use technology and those who do not — even when access is available. Age, habit, and perceived relevance all influence technology use.

items.label: Skill Divide
items.description: The gap between those with the digital skills to use technology effectively and those without. Ranges from basic computer literacy through to advanced technical and information skills.

items.label: Cost Divide
items.description: The gap created by the cost of devices, internet plans, and software. Families with lower incomes may not be able to afford reliable access even where infrastructure exists.

blocks.heading: E-Commerce & Digital Communications
blocks.html:
E-commerce refers to buying and selling goods and services electronically, primarily over the internet. Improved digital communications have fundamentally changed how businesses operate:

24/7 availability: unlike physical stores, e-commerce businesses operate around the clock, reaching customers in any time zone without additional staffing costs.

Global reach: a small Australian business can sell to customers internationally without a physical presence there.

Reduced costs: e-commerce eliminates many overhead costs of physical retail — rent, utilities, and in some cases staffing.

items.label: Direct-to-Consumer (DTC)
items.description: Businesses sell directly to customers through their own website, bypassing wholesalers and retailers. Greater control over the customer experience and higher profit margins.

items.label: Subscription Services
items.description: Customers pay a recurring fee for ongoing access to products or services. Examples: Netflix, Spotify, software-as-a-service (SaaS). Provides predictable, recurring revenue.

items.label: Marketplace Platforms
items.description: Third-party platforms like eBay, Amazon, or Etsy allow businesses to list products for sale to the platform's existing customer base. Lower setup cost but platform fees apply.

blocks.heading: Dependency on Digital Communication
blocks.html:
Modern businesses have become deeply dependent on both electronic and visual communication:

Electronic communication: email, instant messaging, video conferencing, and collaborative platforms (Slack, Teams) have replaced most face-to-face and paper communication in business operations.

Visual communication: infographics, data visualisations, video content, and digital design have become essential business tools. Information is increasingly communicated visually rather than in text alone.

Digital transformation: businesses that have shifted their core operations to digital platforms — cloud-based systems, digital-first communication, e-commerce — are said to have undergone digital transformation.

Cyber issues: dependency on digital systems also creates vulnerability. A cyberattack, system outage, or data breach can halt operations entirely — making cybersecurity a core business priority, not just an IT concern.

question.text: Ransomware is best described as:
options: Malware that encrypts files and demands payment for their release

question.text: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) improves security by:
options: Encrypting all data stored on a computer
Requiring two or more forms of verification before granting access

question.text: Under Australia's Notifiable Data Breaches scheme, an organisation must notify:
options: Affected individuals and the OAIC when a breach may cause serious harm
The OAIC only if the breach affects more than 1000 people

question.text: A hacker calls a company employee, pretending to be from IT support, and asks for their login password. This attack technique is called:
Which of the following is the strongest password?
What is the purpose of a firewall?
options: To encrypt files stored on a hard drive
To monitor and control incoming and outgoing network traffic based on security rules
To back up data automatically to the cloud
To scan emails for spelling errors before sending

question.text: An attacker intercepts and alters communication between two parties without either party knowing. This is known as a:
Which of the following best describes encryption?
options: Deleting sensitive files so they cannot be recovered
Converting data into an unreadable format so only authorised parties with a key can decode it
Scanning files for viruses before opening them
Creating multiple copies of data for backup purposes

question.text: A business receives thousands of requests per second from many computers, crashing its website. What type of attack is this?
Which cybersecurity principle suggests giving users only the minimum access permissions needed to do their job?
Explain what phishing is and describe two measures a business could take to protect its employees from phishing attacks. (6 marks)
question.explanation:
Phishing is a type of cyber attack where criminals send deceptive emails, text messages, or create fake websites that impersonate legitimate organisations (e.g. banks, government agencies, or well-known companies) to trick users into revealing sensitive information such as passwords, credit card numbers, or login credentials. (2 marks)

Measure 1 — Staff training (2 marks): Conduct regular cybersecurity awareness training that teaches employees how to identify suspicious emails (unexpected sender, urgent language, suspicious links, requests for personal information). Run simulated phishing exercises to test and reinforce this knowledge.

Measure 2 — Email filtering (2 marks): Implement an email security gateway that automatically scans incoming emails for known phishing indicators, suspicious links, and malicious attachments before they reach employees' inboxes. This reduces the volume of phishing emails that employees are exposed to.

question.text: A business stores customers' personal information including names, addresses, and payment details. Outline two obligations the business has under the Australian Privacy Principles. (4 marks)
question.explanation:
The business must only collect personal information that is reasonably necessary for its functions, must tell customers why their data is being collected, and must not use or disclose that information for purposes other than those stated (APP 3 and APP 6). (2 marks)

The business must take reasonable steps to protect personal information from misuse, interference, loss, and unauthorised access or modification (APP 11). This includes implementing appropriate security measures such as encryption, access controls, and secure storage. (2 marks)

question.text: A retail business has experienced a ransomware attack. All customer and financial data has been encrypted. Describe the immediate steps the business should take and discuss the legal obligations that arise from this incident. (10 marks)
question.explanation:
Immediate steps (5 marks):
1. Isolate affected systems immediately — disconnect infected computers from the network to prevent the ransomware spreading to other devices and servers.
2. Assess the scope — identify which systems and data have been affected to understand the extent of the breach.
3. Do not pay the ransom — paying does not guarantee data recovery and may fund further criminal activity.
4. Restore from backups — if current, clean backups exist, use these to restore encrypted data.
5. Engage cybersecurity specialists — contact IT security professionals and, where necessary, law enforcement (Australian Cyber Security Centre — ACSC).

Legal obligations (5 marks):
Under the Notifiable Data Breaches (NDB) scheme (Privacy Act 1988), the business must assess whether the breach is likely to result in serious harm to affected individuals. (1 mark) If serious harm is likely (e.g. customer payment details may have been accessed), the business must notify the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC) and all affected customers as soon as practicable. (2 marks) The notification must include details of the breach, the type of information involved, and recommendations for steps individuals can take to protect themselves. (1 mark) Failure to notify when required can result in significant fines from the OAIC. (1 mark)

topics.title: Application Skills
topics.description: Business document creation, spreadsheets, and professional digital communications

blocks.text: In a business context, documents represent the organisation. A poorly formatted letter or inconsistently styled report signals carelessness. Professional document skills are non-negotiable.

items.label: Business Letter
items.description: Formal written communication. Standard structure: sender address, date, recipient address, salutation, body, closing.

items.label: Report
items.description: Presents findings or analysis. Includes headings, executive summary, body, conclusions, and recommendations.

items.label: Memo
items.description: Internal communication between staff. Brief, direct, with clear To/From/Date/Subject header.

items.label: Proposal
items.description: Outlines a plan or solution for a client or manager. Includes problem, solution, timeline, and costs.

blocks.heading: Styles and Formatting
blocks.html:
Styles apply consistent formatting across headings and body text. Using built-in heading styles also enables automatic tables of contents.

Consistent formatting (margins, fonts, spacing) throughout a document signals professionalism.

blocks.heading: Mail merge saves hours
blocks.text: Mail merge lets you send personalised letters to hundreds of recipients from a single template. Create the template with merge fields (e.g. «FirstName», «Address»), connect it to a spreadsheet of recipients, and the software does the rest — 500 personalised letters in minutes.
Spreadsheets are the backbone of business data analysis. Knowing how to build formulas, use functions, and create charts turns raw numbers into business insights.

blocks.title: Learn Basic Excel Formulas in Under 15 Minutes
blocks.caption: SUM, AVERAGE, IF, and more — the formulas every business user needs to know.

blocks.title: SUM — Add a range of values

args.name: number1
args.description: The first range or value to add. Usually a cell range like A1:A10. You can also type individual numbers or cell references.

args.name: number2, ...
args.description: Optional. Additional ranges or values to include. SUM can take up to 255 arguments. Example: =SUM(A1:A10, C1:C5) adds two separate ranges.

blocks.title: Build a SUM formula
blocks.instruction: A business tracks monthly sales in cells B2 through B13 (one row per month). Complete the formula to calculate total annual sales.
blocks.context: January = B2, February = B3, ... December = B13. You need to total all 12 months.

blocks.title: AVERAGE — Calculate the mean of a range

args.name: number1
args.description: The range or values to average. AVERAGE adds all values and divides by the count. Example: AVERAGE(10, 20, 30) returns 20.

args.name: number2, ...
args.description: Optional. Additional ranges. Empty cells are ignored; cells containing 0 are included. Use AVERAGEIF to average only cells meeting a condition.

blocks.title: Build an AVERAGE formula
blocks.instruction: A teacher records student scores in cells D2 through D31 (30 students). Complete the formula to calculate the class average.
blocks.context: Each row represents one student. D2 = Student 1, D3 = Student 2, ... D31 = Student 30.

blocks.title: IF — Return one of two values based on a condition

args.name: logical_test
args.description: The condition to evaluate. Uses comparison operators: = (equals), > (greater than), < (less than), >= (at least), <= (at most), <> (not equal). Example: B2>50 tests whether the value in B2 is greater than 50.

args.name: value_if_true
args.description: What to return if the condition is TRUE. Can be text (in quotes), a number, a formula, or another cell reference. Example: "Pass" returns the word Pass.

args.name: value_if_false
args.description: What to return if the condition is FALSE. Same options as value_if_true. Example: "Fail" returns the word Fail. You can also nest another IF here for multiple conditions.

blocks.title: Build an IF formula
blocks.instruction: A shop gives customers a discount if their order total (in cell E2) is $100 or more. Complete the formula to display "Discount" if the condition is met, or "Full price" if not.
blocks.context: Cell E2 contains the order total. You need to test whether it meets the $100 threshold.

blocks.heading: Formulas and Functions
blocks.html:
Formulas begin with "=" and use cell references: =A1+B1, =SUM(A1:A10), =AVERAGE(B2:B20).

Common functions: SUM (total), AVERAGE (mean), MAX/MIN, COUNT, IF (conditional), VLOOKUP (find matching data).

Charts and graphs visualise spreadsheet data — column charts for comparisons, line charts for trends, pie charts for proportions.

items.label: 📌 Absolute Reference ($A$1)
points: The $ locks the row and column
Does NOT change when the formula is copied
Use when all formulas need to reference the same cell
Example: tax rate stored in A1, used in every row
Write: =$A$1*B2 — copy down, A1 never changes

items.label: ↔️ Relative Reference (A1)
points: Adjusts automatically when formula is copied
Shifts relative to the new cell position
Use for calculations that repeat across rows/columns
Example: =B2*C2 in row 2 becomes =B3*C3 in row 3
The default behaviour in all spreadsheets

blocks.heading: When to use absolute references
blocks.text: If you have a single value (GST rate, exchange rate, target figure) that every formula needs to reference — put it in one cell and use an absolute reference ($A$1). Change that one cell and every formula updates automatically.
How you present and communicate professionally is as important as what you know. Slides should support your message, not overwhelm it. Emails represent you and your organisation.

items.label: The 6×6 Rule
items.description: No more than 6 bullet points per slide, no more than 6 words per bullet. Slides should prompt, not script.

items.label: Slide Master
items.description: Apply a slide master to ensure uniform fonts, colours, and layouts across all slides automatically.

items.label: Presenter Notes
items.description: Put the detail in your notes, not on the slides. Speak to your audience — don't read to them.

items.label: Visual Hierarchy
items.description: One key idea per slide. Large, bold headings. Supporting visuals. White space is your friend.

blocks.heading: Professional Email
blocks.html:
Professional emails need: a clear specific subject line, formal salutation, well-structured paragraphs, and an appropriate closing.

Tone should be professional — avoid abbreviations, slang, and excessive emoji.

Reply All carefully — only use it when all recipients genuinely need to see your response.

Name attachments descriptively: Invoice_March2025_ABCCompany.pdf, not document1.pdf.

items.definition: A feature that personalises a document template for multiple recipients using a data source.
A cell reference that does not change when a formula is copied (e.g. $A$1).
A cell reference that adjusts when a formula is copied (e.g. A1).
A template slide that controls the design of all slides in a presentation.
Rules that restrict what data can be entered into a spreadsheet cell.

blocks.text: Animation and audio software are essential multimedia production tools. Understanding how timelines, tweens, and audio editing work is required for creating interactive and multimedia content in a business context.

items.label: Frame-by-Frame Animation
items.description: Each frame is drawn or positioned manually. Full creative control but time-intensive. Best for complex character animation where every position must be unique.

items.label: Tweening
items.description: Software automatically generates the in-between frames from two keyframes. Motion tweens animate position/rotation/scale; shape tweens morph one shape into another.

items.label: Layers
items.description: Separate elements onto different layers (background, character, text, buttons). Allows independent editing of each element without affecting others.

items.label: Motion Paths
items.description: A guide path that an object follows during animation. Allows natural curved movement rather than straight-line tweens.

items.label: Timeline
items.description: The interface that controls when elements appear, move, and change. Keyframes on the timeline mark where significant changes occur.

items.label: Buttons & Effects
items.description: Interactive buttons respond to user clicks to trigger actions or navigate. Visual effects (fade, blur, colour change) add polish and transitions.

items.label: ✏️ Frame-by-Frame
points: Every frame is manually created
Full creative control over each position
Time-consuming for complex animations
Best for expressive character animation
Result: natural, expressive motion

items.label: 🔄 Tweening
points: Software generates frames between keyframes
Set start and end state; software fills in between
Much faster to produce than frame-by-frame
Best for logos, UI transitions, simple movement
Result: clean, consistent motion

blocks.heading: Audio Editing Features
blocks.html:
Audio editing software (Audacity, Adobe Audition, GarageBand) allows manipulation of recorded or imported sound.

Core editing tools: Trim/cut — remove unwanted sections. Copy/paste — duplicate or rearrange audio clips. Fade in/out — gradually increase or decrease volume at the start or end of a clip.

Effects: Normalise (adjust overall volume to a consistent level), noise reduction (remove background hiss), reverb/echo, pitch adjustment, equalisation (EQ — boost or reduce specific frequency ranges).

Converting formats: audio can be exported in different formats — WAV for high-quality archiving, MP3 for compressed sharing, AAC for web/iOS use.

Exporting: choose the appropriate format, bit rate (higher = better quality but larger file size), and sample rate (44.1 kHz is CD standard).

blocks.text: Building websites and collaborating digitally are core professional skills. Understanding HTML/CSS structure, responsive design, and online collaboration tools is expected of all business professionals working in a digital environment.

items.label: 🏗️ HTML (Structure)
points: HyperText Markup Language
Defines the content and structure of a page

Uses tags:

,

, , ,
Sets out what elements exist on the page
Saved as .html files

points: Cascading Style Sheets
Controls appearance: colour, font, layout, spacing
Uses selectors and properties: h1 { color: blue; }
Can be in a separate .css file or embedded in HTML
Saved as .css files (or within the HTML file)

blocks.heading: Web Authoring Features
blocks.html:
Web authoring tools (from code editors to visual builders like WordPress) provide features for building complete websites:

Hyperlinks: anchor tags link pages together and to external websites. Good navigation relies on well-organised, descriptive hyperlinks.

Graphics: images must be optimised for web (72 PPI, appropriate format — JPEG for photos, PNG for transparency, SVG for icons). Alt text provides accessibility descriptions for screen readers.

Templates: pre-built page layouts and CSS style files apply consistent design across an entire website. Changing the template file updates all pages simultaneously.

blocks.heading: Responsive Design
blocks.html:
A responsive website adapts its layout to fit different screen sizes — from a desktop monitor to a smartphone.

Flexible layouts: use percentage-based widths rather than fixed pixel widths, so elements resize proportionally with the screen.

CSS Grid and Flexbox: modern layout systems that automatically reflow content to fit available screen space.

Flexible images: images scale within their containers using max-width: 100% in CSS.

Media queries: CSS rules that apply different styles based on screen width. Example: @media (max-width: 600px) { ... } applies mobile-specific styles when the screen is narrow.

items.label: Project Management Tools
items.description: Manage tasks, deadlines, and team roles. Examples: Trello, Asana, Monday.com. Allow teams to assign tasks, track progress, and identify blockers.

items.label: Document Collaboration
items.description: Multiple users edit the same document simultaneously with real-time saving. Examples: Google Docs, Microsoft 365. Version history allows rollback if mistakes are made.

items.label: Communication Tools
items.description: Team messaging and video conferencing. Examples: Slack, Microsoft Teams, Zoom. Replace or supplement email for rapid, threaded team communication.

items.label: Pseudo-Social Networking
items.description: Internal social platforms for organisations. Examples: Yammer, LinkedIn (professional network). Allow informal idea sharing and updates across large teams.

items.definition: HyperText Markup Language — defines the structure and content of web pages.
Cascading Style Sheets — controls the visual appearance of web pages.
A design approach that adapts a website's layout to different screen sizes.
A CSS rule that applies different styles depending on screen size or device type.
An automatically generated animation between two keyframes.
A specific point in time on an animation timeline where a defined state is set.
The number of animation frames displayed per second; higher rates produce smoother motion.

question.text: A student writes the formula =SUM($A$1:$A$10) and copies it to another cell. The cell reference $A$1:$A$10 will:
options: Adjust relative to the new cell position
Convert to a relative reference automatically

question.text: Which feature allows a business to send personalised letters to hundreds of customers from a single template?
The "6×6 rule" in presentations recommends:
options: Six slides per presentation, six minutes each
Six fonts and six colours per slide
No more than six bullet points per slide and six words per bullet
Six images and six headings per slide

question.text: Which word processing feature automatically adjusts the formatting style of all headings across a long document with a single change?
A spreadsheet formula reads =IF(B2>100,"Bonus","No bonus"). What will the cell display if B2 contains the value 85?
In a professional business email, which element should appear in the closing before your name?
options: A formal sign-off (e.g. "Kind regards," or "Yours sincerely,")
The date and time of writing

question.text: A student uses the =AVERAGE(C2:C20) function. What will this formula calculate?
options: The highest value in the range C2 to C20
The total of all values in the range C2 to C20
The arithmetic mean of all values in the range C2 to C20

question.text: Which presentation design principle states that repeating a visual element (colour, shape, or font) throughout a presentation creates visual cohesion?
A business letter must include a professional salutation. Which of the following is the most appropriate opening if you know the recipient's name is Jordan Smith?
In a spreadsheet, what does the COUNTA function count?
options: Only blank cells in a range
All non-empty cells in a range (including text and numbers)
Only cells that meet a specified condition

question.text: Explain the difference between an absolute and a relative cell reference in a spreadsheet, and give an example of when you would use an absolute reference. (4 marks)
question.explanation:
A relative cell reference (e.g. A1) adjusts automatically when a formula is copied to another cell, shifting the reference to maintain the same relative position. For example, if =A1*B1 in row 1 is copied to row 2, it becomes =A2*B2. (2 marks)

An absolute cell reference (e.g. $A$1) is fixed and does not change when copied. An example of when to use an absolute reference: if a tax rate is stored in cell A1 and you want to multiply every sales value in column B by that rate, you would write =$A$1*B1 and copy down — the A1 reference stays fixed while the B reference adjusts for each row. (2 marks)

question.text: A business needs to send a personalised letter to 500 customers informing them of a new product. Describe how the business would use mail merge to produce these letters efficiently. (4 marks)
question.explanation:
Step 1: Create a data source (spreadsheet or database) containing each customer's details in separate columns — e.g. FirstName, LastName, Address, City. Each row represents one customer. (1 mark)

Step 2: Create a letter template document in the word processor with merge fields inserted where personalised data should appear (e.g. "Dear <<FirstName>> <<LastName>>"). (1 mark)

Step 3: Connect the template to the data source using the mail merge function and preview the merged letters. (1 mark)

Step 4: Complete the merge to produce 500 individual letters, each containing the personalised details for one customer. The letters can be printed or saved as individual files. (1 mark)

question.text: A small business owner uses a spreadsheet to track monthly sales. The spreadsheet records the product name, units sold, and unit price for each transaction. Describe how the owner could use spreadsheet features to analyse this data and present a summary to stakeholders. (8 marks)
question.explanation:
Formulas and calculations (2 marks): The owner could add a "Total Revenue" column using a formula (=Units_Sold * Unit_Price) for each row. A SUM formula at the bottom could calculate total monthly revenue. AVERAGE, MAX, and MIN functions could identify the average sale value, best-performing product, and slowest-selling item.

Sorting and filtering (2 marks): Sort the data by "Total Revenue" (descending) to identify the top-selling products at a glance. Apply filters to isolate specific products or date ranges for more focused analysis.

Pivot table or summary table (2 marks): Use a PivotTable to automatically summarise total units sold and total revenue by product category, allowing the owner to quickly see which categories are performing best without manually writing formulas.

Chart for stakeholder presentation (2 marks): Create a column chart or pie chart showing the revenue contribution of each product category. Charts communicate trends and comparisons more effectively than raw numbers for non-technical stakeholders. Include a clear title, labelled axes, and a legend. Export or embed the chart in a presentation for the stakeholder meeting.

topics.title: Project Management
topics.description: Systems development, requirements analysis, and digital solution design

blocks.text: Building software without a structured process is like constructing a building without blueprints. The SDLC is the framework professionals use to take a client's problem through to a working digital solution.

blocks.title: Software Development Life Cycle Explained
blocks.caption: A clear walkthrough of all seven SDLC phases — from planning to maintenance.

items.label: 1. Planning
items.description: Define the scope, feasibility, and resources required. Is the project worth doing?

items.label: 2. Analysis
items.description: Gather and document what the system must do. Produce a requirements specification.

items.label: 3. Design
items.description: Create technical specifications — database design, UI wireframes, system architecture.

items.label: 4. Implementation
items.description: Build the solution — write code, create databases, design interfaces.

items.label: 5. Testing
items.description: Verify the system works correctly and meets all requirements before release.

items.label: 6. Deployment
items.description: Release the system to users. Migrate data, train staff, go live.

items.label: 💧 Waterfall
points: Sequential — each phase must complete before the next
Well-documented with clear milestones
Best for projects with fixed, well-understood requirements
Changes are costly once a phase is complete
Example: government systems, infrastructure projects

items.label: 🔄 Agile
points: Iterative — work in short sprints (1–4 weeks)
Continuous feedback from the client
Best when requirements may change or are unclear
Delivers working software early and often
Example: startups, web apps, mobile development

blocks.text: Getting requirements right is the most important phase of the SDLC. Misunderstood requirements mean building the wrong thing — no matter how well it's built, it won't solve the client's problem.

items.label: ⚙️ Functional Requirements
points: Describe what the system must DO
Specific features and behaviours
Example: "Users must be able to log in with email and password"
Example: "The system must send a confirmation email after checkout"
Tested by checking if the function exists and works

items.label: 📊 Non-functional Requirements
points: Describe HOW the system should perform
Quality attributes: speed, reliability, security, usability
Example: "Pages must load within 3 seconds"
Example: "The system must be available 99.9% of the time"
Tested by performance measurement and benchmarking

blocks.heading: Gathering Requirements
blocks.html:
Requirements are gathered through: interviews with stakeholders (the client, managers, end users), surveys, observation of existing systems and workflows, and analysis of current documentation.

The output is a requirements specification — a document the client signs off on before design begins.

blocks.heading: Always get sign-off
blocks.text: A requirements specification signed by the client protects both parties. If the client later says "but I wanted X" and X isn't in the signed document, you have evidence. Without sign-off, scope creep and disputes are almost inevitable.

Design documentation translates requirements into technical blueprints. These documents guide developers, database designers, and UI designers — and prove to the client that the solution has been thought through.

items.label: System Flowchart
items.description: Shows the flow of data through a system — processes, inputs, outputs, and storage.

items.label: Data Flow Diagram (DFD)
items.description: Shows how data moves between processes, data stores, and external entities.

items.label: Entity-Relationship Diagram (ERD)
items.description: Shows the relationships between data entities in a database design. Used to plan the database structure.

items.label: Wireframe
items.description: Low-fidelity sketches showing the layout and structure of user interfaces before development begins.

blocks.heading: Using Each Tool Effectively
blocks.html:
Wireframes are produced first — before any coding. They show the layout of screens (buttons, text fields, menus, navigation) without colour or styling. Show wireframes to the client and get sign-off before designing the visual interface.

ERDs are drawn during database design. Identify the entities (things you need to store data about — Customer, Product, Order), their attributes (fields), and the relationships between them (one customer can place many orders). Use standard notation: rectangles for entities, ovals for attributes, diamonds for relationships.

System flowcharts and DFDs are used to document process flows. Flowcharts use standard symbols: rectangles (processes), diamonds (decisions), parallelograms (input/output), cylinders (data storage). A DFD uses circles for processes, rectangles for external entities, open rectangles for data stores, and arrows for data flows.

Together, these documents form the design specification — a complete blueprint for the system that the development team builds from.

items.label: 🔄 System Flowchart
points: Shows the sequence of steps and decisions in a process
Focus: HOW the system works step by step
Uses decision diamonds (yes/no branches)
Good for documenting algorithms and procedures
Audience: developers and technical staff

items.label: 📊 Data Flow Diagram (DFD)
points: Shows WHERE data comes from, goes to, and is stored
Focus: the FLOW of data through the system
No decision logic — only data movement
Good for showing how entities interact with the system
Audience: analysts, clients, and system designers

blocks.heading: Design before you build
blocks.text: The purpose of design documentation is to make decisions on paper, where changes are cheap. Changing a wireframe takes minutes; changing a coded interface takes days. Get client approval on designs before touching code.
Testing is how you prove the system works. Without systematic testing, bugs and unmet requirements only surface after deployment — when fixing them is expensive and embarrassing.

items.label: Unit Testing
items.description: Tests individual components or functions in isolation. Finds problems at the source before they compound.

items.label: Integration Testing
items.description: Tests that components work correctly together. Catches interface problems between modules.

items.label: UAT
items.description: User Acceptance Testing — the client or end users verify the system meets their requirements in real conditions.

items.label: Black Box Testing
items.description: Tests functionality without knowing the internal code. Focuses on inputs and expected outputs.

blocks.heading: Test Plans
blocks.html:
A test plan documents: what will be tested, the test data to be used, the expected result, and the actual result.

Every function and edge case should have a corresponding test. Good test data includes valid inputs, invalid inputs, and boundary values.

blocks.heading: Test with invalid data too
blocks.text: Don't just test that the system works when users do the right thing. Test what happens when they enter letters in a number field, leave required fields empty, or try to book a date in the past. A robust system handles all of these gracefully.

items.definition: Systems Development Life Cycle — a structured process for building IT systems.
A specific capability or behaviour the system must provide.
A quality attribute such as performance, security, or reliability.
User Acceptance Testing — testing by end users to verify the system meets requirements.
A low-fidelity visual layout showing the structure of a user interface.

question.text: "The system must allow customers to reset their password via email" is an example of a:

Which SDLC phase involves gathering and documenting what the system must do?
What is the key characteristic of the Agile SDLC model?
options: All requirements are fully documented before any development begins
Development occurs in short, iterative cycles (sprints) with continuous client feedback
The system is built, then handed to the client with no further changes allowed
Testing only occurs after the entire system is built

question.text: Which SDLC model is best suited to a project where requirements are very well understood and unlikely to change?
A developer writes a test to check that a single function called "calculateTax" produces the correct output. What type of testing is this?
What document produced during the Analysis phase formally defines what the system must do and acts as an agreement between the client and developer?
"The system must handle 1000 simultaneous users without performance degradation" is an example of a:
During the Maintenance phase of the SDLC, which activity is most likely to occur?
options: Creating wireframes for the user interface
Releasing patches to fix bugs discovered after deployment
Conducting user acceptance testing before go-live

question.text: An entity-relationship diagram (ERD) is most commonly used during which SDLC phase?
Distinguish between functional and non-functional requirements. Provide one example of each for a business ordering system. (4 marks)
question.explanation:
Functional requirements define specific behaviours or functions the system must perform — what it must do. Example: "The ordering system must allow customers to add items to a shopping cart and proceed to checkout." (2 marks)

Non-functional requirements define how the system should perform or the quality attributes it must have. Example: "The ordering system must process payment confirmation within 5 seconds" (performance) or "The system must be available 24/7 with 99.9% uptime" (reliability). (2 marks)

question.text: A business is developing a new inventory management system. Describe how a test plan would be used during the testing phase of the SDLC. (4 marks)
question.explanation: A test plan is a document that outlines the testing approach for the system. (1 mark) For the inventory system, it would list each function to be tested (e.g. adding stock, removing stock, generating low-stock alerts), along with specific test data to use for each test (e.g. adding -5 units to test validation). (1 mark) For each test, the expected result and the actual result are recorded. (1 mark) If the actual result differs from the expected result, the discrepancy is logged as a defect for the development team to fix before the system can be approved for deployment. The test plan provides a systematic record proving the system has been thoroughly verified. (1 mark)

question.text: A small business wants to replace its paper-based booking system with a digital solution. You have been asked to manage this project. Describe how you would approach the Analysis and Design phases of the SDLC for this project. (10 marks)
question.explanation:
Analysis phase (5 marks):
Requirements gathering: Interview the business owner and staff to understand how the current paper system works, what its limitations are, and what they need the digital system to do. Observe the current booking process in action to identify all steps involved.

Functional requirements examples: The system must allow staff to create, view, modify, and cancel bookings; the system must send automated email reminders to customers 24 hours before their appointment; the system must prevent double-booking the same time slot.

Non-functional requirements examples: The system must be accessible from any device via a web browser; the system must load booking pages within 2 seconds; staff must be able to learn the system with less than 2 hours of training.

Documentation: Produce a requirements specification document that the business owner signs off on before design begins, to confirm shared understanding of scope.

Design phase (5 marks):
User interface design: Create wireframes for the main screens — the booking calendar view, the new booking form, and the customer details page. Present these to the business owner for feedback before development begins.
